coredata.dc:description: Bacterial infection continues to be one of the biggest threats to human health. The therapeutic effect is significantly reduced as a result of the development of super-bacteria-resistant bacteria due to the overuse of antibiotics for conventional antimicrobial treatment. The emergence of drug-resistant bacteria makes it necessary to develop new antimicrobial treatments. Following the successful application of photothermal therapy in the field of oncology treatment, more and more researchers are applying it to antimicrobial treatments and achieving important results, especially in the treatment of drug-resistant bacterial infections. As a non-invasive anti-infection method, photothermal therapy has the advantages of broad-spectrum antimicrobial properties, a short treatment period, and low systemic impact. In the PTT, the antimicrobial efficacy strongly depends on the different laser properties and the choice of photothermal agents (PTAs). The purpose of this review is to discuss the most recent developments in photothermal nanomaterials that are antibacterial. We will delve into the antimicrobial principles and antimicrobial spectrum of different PTAs and provide a summary of current developments in synthesis, classification, structural features, physicochemical properties, and antibacterial performance research. The challenges and future prospects of enhanced antimicrobial performance and reduced cytotoxicity of PTAs are investigated.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Antibacterial mechanism of photothermal nanomaterials and the significance of combined antibacterial strategy.

row.entry: Absorb near-infrared light and generating a localized hyperthermal environment through the photothermal effect damages bacterial membranes, denaturing proteins, inducing protein leakage, and causing irreversible harm to microorganisms.
The strategy of specific binding or targeted aggregation is employed to minimize detrimental effects on healthy cells and enhance sterilization efficiency.
The utilization of specific binding or targeted aggregation serves as a carrier for delivering antimicrobial agents to the intended focal area.
The promotion of tissue regeneration in the vicinity of infected lesions and the enhancement of local resistance to infection are essential for facilitating the healing process.

Nanocomposites undergo targeted binding or aggregation, after which photothermal nanomaterials loaded with antimicrobial drugs are activated by near-infrared light for controlled release, leading to efficient local antimicrobial activity.
Physical contact with bacteria enables capture, while the unique shape characteristics of the material physically damage bacteria and puncture bacterial biofilms
Under near-infrared light irradiation, the material generates reactive oxygen species, leading to oxidative damage to bacteria.
Enhancement of antibiotics and antibacterial peptide efficacy

Combining antibiotics with photothermal nanomaterials significantly enhances antibacterial performance compared to antibiotics alone. The combination of the antibacterial peptide Buforin 2b (BF2b) with photothermal nanomaterials demonstrates increased affinity for drug-resistant bacteria.
Stimulation of cytokine and vascular factor release.
The release of factors, including VEGF, HIF-1α, CD31 and CD68, promotes immunomodulatory functions, angiogenesis, and the aggregation of immune cells.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacterial infection continues to be one of the biggest threats to human health. The therapeutic effect is significantly reduced as a result of the development of super-bacteria-resistant bacteria due to the overuse of antibiotics for conventional antimicrobial treatment. The emergence of drug-resistant bacteria makes it necessary to develop new antimicrobial treatments. Following the successful application of photothermal therapy in the field of oncology treatment, more and more researchers are applying it to antimicrobial treatments and achieving important results, especially in the treatment of drug-resistant bacterial infections. As a non-invasive anti-infection method, photothermal therapy has the advantages of broad-spectrum antimicrobial properties, a short treatment period, and low systemic impact. In the PTT, the antimicrobial efficacy strongly depends on the different laser properties and the choice of photothermal agents (PTAs). The purpose of this review is to discuss the most recent developments in photothermal nanomaterials that are antibacterial. We will delve into the antimicrobial principles and antimicrobial spectrum of different PTAs and provide a summary of current developments in synthesis, classification, structural features, physicochemical properties, and antibacterial performance research. The challenges and future prospects of enhanced antimicrobial performance and reduced cytotoxicity of PTAs are investigated.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infections have long been a challenging problem due to the high adaptability and strong proliferative ability of bacteria [1,2]. Antibiotic application is a common therapeutic method to combat bacterial infections, but the overuse of broad-spectrum antibiotics has accelerated the emergence of drug-resistant microorganisms [3,4]. In recent years, the therapeutic effect of antibiotics has been significantly reduced due to the emergence of super-bacteria-resistant bacteria [5]. Despite extensive clinical and basic research endeavors over a prolonged period, treating multidrug-resistant illnesses remains a formidable challenge. Millions of patients die each year due to lethal drug-resistant microbes, such as Mycobacterium tuberculosis (M. tuberculosis, Mtb), Escherichia coli (E. coli), and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), among others [6–8]. Many other techniques natural antimicrobial substances, including vaccinations and phage-based treatments, have been employed to get rid of bacteria, but because of their weak antimicrobial activities, they are not a workable alternative to manufactured antibiotics [9,10]. With the onset of the “post-antibiotic era,” the development of new antibacterial agents to substitute for antibiotics has become an urgent need. The rapid progress of nanotechnology has shown promising potential for the treatment of bacterial infections, owing to the small size, large surface-to-volume ratio, and unique physical and chemical properties of nanomaterials [11,12]. Compared to traditional antibiotics, nanomaterials exhibit improved interactions with bacteria [13]. Nanomaterials utilized for antimicrobial applications must possess the ability to accumulate locally, which requires great permeability and local aggregation or disruption of local vasculature. And/or, they should be able to bind to targets within the diseased tissue, such as through antigen–antibody or receptor-ligand binding, allowing the drug to be used systemically yet act locally [14]. Nanomaterials that exhibit excellent biocompatibility and stability can enhance the delivery of antimicrobial agents by improving their permeability, targeted antimicrobial properties, and controllability, thereby increasing the accumulation of antibiotics in the diseased area at lower doses, and subsequently reducing the incidence of adverse reactions to antimicrobials [15]. Photothermal therapy (PTT) is a non-invasive method that offers several advantages, including short treatment periods, minimal overall impact on the body, low cost, good selectivity, low toxicity, high controllability, localized application, and high delivery efficiency [16–18]. Given the success of photothermal therapy (PTT) in anticancer treatment, an increasing number of researchers are exploring the potential of PTT as a novel approach for antimicrobial therapy against bacteria [19]. Photothermal therapies (PTTs) based on photothermal agents (PTAs), mainly photothermal nanomaterials, have recently gained popularity as a potent treatment for controlling bacterial infections [20]. A variety of nanomaterials, including carbon-based nanomaterials (CBNs), noble metal nanoparticles (NPs), semiconductor NPs, conjugated CuS NPs, polymeric nanostructures, Au nanostructures, gold NPs, antibacterial polypeptides, and iron oxide (FeO), have been used as PTAs for antibacterial applications [21,22]. These nanomaterials possess plasmonic properties, optical conductivity, energy bandgap, free electron pairs, conductance, optical wavelength, and morphological qualities that make them suitable for use as PTAs [23]. The ability of a nanomaterial to convert light into heat is largely dependent on its nanostructure, which includes particle shape, size, crystal structure, and morphological characteristics [24]. The amount of heat produced by PTAs is influenced by several laser light characteristics, including wavelength, power density (total energy per second delivered into a specific area), frequency of irradiations, laser range, and time. PTT in the near-infrared (NIR) spectral region has been shown to achieve significantly higher photothermal conversion efficiencies and greater target penetration, resulting in improved antimicrobial efficacy [25,26]. The use of PTTs based on PTAs holds great promise for the development of effective and safe antibacterial therapies. Our study aims to conduct a systematic review of the latest advancements in PTAs reported in recent years, as well as provide examples of their effective antimicrobial applications. Additionally, we provide a detailed description of the morphological structure, physicochemical properties, synthesis techniques, and antimicrobial properties of PTAs. We suggest the combined use of nano-PTAs with other antimicrobial functional materials to enhance antimicrobial activity, reduce cytotoxicity, or both. Our objective is to encourage the development of effective antibacterial strategies utilizing nano-PTAs.

Photothermal therapy (PTT), based on photothermal agents (PTAs), has emerged as a promising approach for cancer treatment, and its potential application in antimicrobial therapy has also been investigated [27]. In particular, nanosystem-based photothermal antimicrobials have been proposed as a potential solution to the growing problem of multi-drug resistant bacteria [28]. Nanosystem-based photothermal antimicrobials are of particular interest in this review. PTT is based on photothermal agents (PTAs) that convert light absorption into heat, resulting in high temperatures that cause protein denaturation or thermal ablation of bacteria and cancer cells [29]. In addition, to quickly destroying bacterial structure and functions, the light-transferred heat can also strengthen the immune system’s defenses against intracellular bacterial infections [30,31]. The surface properties of nanomaterials can influence their photothermal effects as well as other physical and chemical properties [24,32]. The surface properties of nanomaterials can influence their photothermal effects as well as other physical and chemical properties. The PTT-mediated bactericidal effect is indirectly influenced by the physicochemical properties of the surface of PTAs. For example, changing the surface hydrophobicity of nanoparticles can increase the bactericidal potency of PTT and promote bacterial biofilm breakdown [33,34]. After activation of photothermal agents (PATs) using a near-infrared (NIR) laser, their surface plasma bands convert electronic radiation into heat [35]. The photothermal conversion process generates heat, which leads to high temperatures that can sterilize. There are different types of antimicrobial mechanisms of the photothermal effect, including the inactivation of proteins and intracellular protein leakage [36,37]. The therapeutic effects of PTT are dependent upon the type of PTAs used. In recent decades, several generations have continued to search for functionally applicable PTAs. Photothermally active nanoparticles (PANs) are the most representative class of antimicrobial PTAs in the field of antibacterial research. By subjecting PANs to light ranging from the UV spectrum to the NIR spectra with 650–900 nm wavelength, temperature rise and thermal relaxation can be achieved [38]. Near-infrared (NIR) light with wavelengths between 700 and 1400 nm can penetrate human tissue with minimal damage to healthy cells, and this occurs within the safe NIR windows [39,40]. (Fig. 1 ) Notably, many photothermal nanomaterials exhibit strong absorption of NIR light. Photothermally active nanoparticles (PANs) are able to efficiently convert NIR light into heat, which results in a localized increase in temperature that can disrupt bacterial membranes, denature proteins, cause protein leakage, and irreversibly damage microorganisms. This mechanism of action demonstrates the potential of PANs to serve as effective antimicrobial agents [16,38].

The superior structural and physicochemical characteristics of carbon-based nanomaterials (CBNs) endow them with high antibacterial activity, moderate biocompatibility, and eco-friendly nature, making them particularly attractive for use in antibacterial treatment [41]. CBNs exhibit distinct optical, electrical, and thermal stability properties due to the different allotropes of carbon that can be synthesized through the interactions of carbon atoms. CBNs include various types, such as 0-dimensional (0D) nanodiamonds (NDS), fullerene, carbon dots (C-dots), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbonized polymeric dots (CPDs), as well as 1-dimensional (1D) carbon nanotubes (CNTs), two-dimensional (2D) graphene and its derivatives, and graphitic carbon nitride (g-CN) [42–48]. (Fig. 2 ) And The majority of carbon atoms in carbon nanotubes, fullerene, graphene, and its derivatives are sp2-hybridized, while most nanodiamonds are composed of carbon atoms that have undergone sp3 hybridization. Carbon dots, including graphene quantum dots, have a combination of defects and heteroatoms in addition to sp2 and sp3-hybridized carbon atoms. Carbon and nitrogen atoms sp2 hybridize to form graphite planes, which then undergo π-conjugation to produce g-CN [49,50].

In their pristine form, most CBNs exhibit weak in vitro/vivo bacterial selectivity and moderate anti-biofilm activity. To reduce damage to healthy cells, CBNs should be combined with additional surface functional groups or nanomaterials capable of targeting bacteria and combating bacterial biofilms more effectively [51,52]. Wang et al., demonstrated that combining silver nanoparticles with magnetic graphene (GBNs) can produce increased antibacterial activity due to the synergistic effects of CBNs. The photothermal effect of GBNs denatures bacteria and controllably releases Ag+ into the bacteria, achieving effective sterilization. However, the physical and chemical characteristics of CBNs have a significant impact on their targeting effectiveness and anti-bacterial biofilm efficacy [53]. The anti-biofilm mechanism and capabilities of CBNs with different dimensionality exhibit discernible variations when compared to CBNs with a similar degree of carbon orbital hybridization. Other factors, including frame, lateral sizes, surface functional groups, doping atoms, and density of surface charges, can also affect the anti-biofilm characteristics of CBNs of the same dimensionality [51,54]. Previous studies have indicated that the primary method for enhancing the anti-biofilm efficacy of carbon-based nanomaterials (CBNs) is to modify their physicochemical properties by adjusting various synthesis parameters. These parameters may include surface functionalization of the nanomaterial, synthesis environment, choice of synthetic precursors, and purification strategies, among others. By optimizing these factors, it may be possible to improve the efficacy of CBNs for combatting biofilms and other related applications [47,55,56]. Leigh et al., synthesized various carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from gentamicin at different temperatures and evaluated their antibacterial activity against various bacterial strains [55]. The findings revealed that CQD180 exhibited potent antibacterial properties against both planktonic and biofilm-forming Staphylococcus aureus strains, while demonstrating low mammalian cytotoxicity and minimal drug resistance. Furthermore, in addition to retaining the intrinsic antibacterial properties of gentamicin, CQD180 exhibited the ability to penetrate bacterial membranes and stimulate intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) production, ultimately resulting in rapid bacterial death. Lin et al., synthesized carbon dots-LP (CDs-LP) from Lactobacillus plantarum (L. plantarum) LLC-605 via a one-step hydrothermal carbonation process, and evaluated their efficacy against E. coli biofilm formation [57]. The resulting CDs-LP exhibited significant inhibition of E. coli biofilm formation, without impeding the growth of E. coli and mammalian cells. This unique characteristic of CDs-LP resulted in a reduction of cytotoxicity and bacterial resistance development, thereby demonstrating CDs-LP’s potential as a promising treatment option for infections caused by E. coli. Carbon-based nanoparticles have been shown to influence the lipid membrane extraction effect on bacterial cells, oxidative stress (both ROS-dependent and ROS-independent stress), photosensitizers under photoexcitation (ROS-photo-dependent sterilization), inhibition of bacterial physiological metabolism, and bacterial wrapping [58]. CBNs can absorb more light and generate a modest number of fluorescent quantum with a higher level of photothermal conversion when exposed to photoexcitation [59]. A summary of the antibacterial properties of various types of carbon-based nanomaterials is shown in Table 1 .

Nanodiamonds (NDs) consist of a carbon core that has undergone SP3 hybridization and is surrounded by an oxygen-containing functional group on the surface [69]. These nanodiamond particles are typically formed with an average size ranging from 2 to 10 nm when oxygen-deficient explosives, such as TNT or hexogen, detonate in an inert medium. During the detonation process, the incomplete combustion of explosives that serve as both energy and carbon sources leads to soot production. This soot contains a high percentage, up to 80%, of microscopic diamond crystallites measuring approximately 4–5 nm in size [70]. There are three primary mechanisms by which explosions generate diamond nanoparticles. The first method, known as the “Du Pont method,” involves compressing carbon precursors within a capsule using an external shock wave with a pressure of 140 GPa, resulting in diamond particles formation. The second approach entails the detonation of a mixture containing explosive(s) and carbon-containing material(s), leading to nanodiamond production. The third technique relies only on the explosives themselves to create diamond clusters from carbon atoms, requiring no additional precursor materials [70]. These diverse methods offer different routes for nanodiamond synthesis, allowing for flexibility in their production depending on the specific requirements and desired applications.

Nanodiamonds (NDs) are carbon nanoparticles with a predominantly diamond-like structure, making them highly attractive for medical applications. These materials offer a range of desirable properties, including extensive surface functionalization, superior mechanical strength, high biocompatibility, large surface area, non-toxicity, colloidal stability, chemical inertness, hardness, and optical characteristics [49,71]. Pristine NDs exhibit photoluminescence and fluorescence properties, making them particularly interesting for various applications [72].In terms of biocompatibility, NDs have been shown to outperform other carbon nanomaterials such as single-walled and multi-walled carbon nanotubes, exhibiting the least toxic effects on cell survival in vitro [73]. However, pristine carbon-based nanomaterials (CBNs) have limited efficacy against biofilms and low targeted toxicity against bacteria. CBNs' targeting efficiency and antibiofilm effectiveness are significantly influenced by their physical and chemical properties [52]. Ryu et al., developed nanoparticles by mixing phase-change material, chlorin e6, and NDs in an organic solvent [74]. By adjusting the duration of laser irradiation, they could control the release of chlorin e6 (Ce6) and modify the quantity and timing of its release. This approach aims to minimize side effects and optimize the therapeutic course for more effective eradication of focal infection tissues. Such research endeavors highlight the potential of utilizing NDs in medical applications, wherein their unique properties can be harnessed to enhance therapeutic outcomes while ensuring minimal adverse effects on biological systems.

The four primary types of carbon dots (C-Dots) include carbon nanodots (CNDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and carbonized polymeric dots (CPDs) [75]. C-Dots, characterized as globular carbon nanoparticles resulting from graphitic fragmentation, possess diameters smaller than ten nm and exhibit carbon-based fluorescent properties. Furthermore, C-Dots encompass additional subgroups, such as amorphous carbon dots and graphite dots [76]. In CQDs, which are composed of single or multilayer nanographene, the size of the π-conjugated structure and the presence of edge/surface chemical functional groups or structural flaws between graphene layers dictate their optical absorbance and other optical characteristics [77,78]. The multiple-layer graphite nanostructure is evident in the spherical cores of CQDs. The photoluminescence (PL) emission capabilities of CQDs are mainly governed by the size-dependent quantum confinement phenomenon and the inherent state of fluorescence [75,79]. On the other hand, CPDs exhibit hybrid structures comprising polymer chains and aggregated/cross-linked carbon cores, with their optical characteristics primarily influenced by molecular state and cross-link architectures [80,81]. ( Fig. 3
A) The manufacturing of CDs generally involves the suspension of tiny carbon nanoparticles in water or another liquid, utilizing either carbon compounds or organics as precursors [82,83]. GQDs are typically synthesized through a “top-down” approach, involving the exfoliation and slicing of carbon materials like powdered graphite, charcoal black, activated charcoal, CNTs, carbon ash, and carbon fibers. Various methods such as acid chemical exfoliation, arc transfer electrons, electrochemical oxidation, ultrasound synthesis, and laser ablation can be employed for this purpose [83–85]. C-Dots possess a range of desirable properties, including passivated surfaces, UV–visible absorption, fluorescence, photoinduced electron transfer (PET), high photostability, antibacterial activity, solution dispersibility, compatibility with organic and carbon-based materials, photocatalytic functionality, and phototoxicity (ROS generation under laser-induced oxidative stress leading to targeted cell destruction). C-Dots' excellent hydrophilicity, stability, and biocompatibility contribute to their low cytotoxicity [86,87]. ( Fig. 3 B) As a result, C-Dots find extensive applications in biosensory, drug delivery, bioimaging, bio-detection, functional materials, photodynamic therapy, photocatalysis, and antibacterial agents [18,87–90]. Liu et al., successfully synthesized plasmonic gold nanodendrites (AuNDs) mediated by carbon dots (CDs), possessing notable near-infrared (NIR-II) absorption capabilities [91]. These AuNDs demonstrated broadband absorption spanning from 300 to 1350 nm, coupled with a remarkable photothermal conversion efficiency of 38.0%. The application of AuNDs in photothermal therapy (PTT) yielded promising outcomes, effectively eradicating tumor cells and offering a fresh perspective on harnessing photothermal antimicrobial activity. Moniruzzaman et al., conducted a study investigating the antibacterial properties of PY-CDs (pyrene-based carbon dots) [60]. The results of their study revealed that PY-CDs were effective in killing bacteria through the amplification of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in combination with the thermal effect. Additionally, the PY-CDs demonstrated exceptional near-infrared (NIR) light absorption. Liu et al., in vitro antibacterial experiments were performed using Fe-CDs (iron-based carbon dots) [61]. The results of these experiments indicated that Fe-CDs, when combined with local heat, generated significant amounts of ROS and exhibited nearly perfect antibacterial efficacy against both Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli [61]. (Fig. 3 C-F) The combination of Fe-CDs and NIR irradiation also demonstrated additional benefits, such as healing bacteria-infected wounds. These studies highlight carbon dots, specifically PY-CDs and Fe-CDs, as effective antibacterial agents. Their ability to generate ROS, in conjunction with their responsiveness to NIR light, makes them promising candidates for combating bacterial infections. Further research in this area could contribute to the development of advanced treatments for bacterial infections and wound healing.

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are seamless cylinders made of graphene sheets that have a high aspect ratio (ratio between length and diameter) withlengths of many micrometers and diameters as small as 1 nm [92]. The unique physicochemical characteristics of CNTs, including exceptionally high tensile strength, high electrical conductivity, excellent ductility, and relatively low chemical reactivity, are widely recognized [93]. Based on the number of graphitic layers, CNTs can be categorized into three types: single-walled CNTs (SWNTs), double-walled CNTs (DWNTs), and multi-walled CNTs (MWNTs). SWCNTs are formed by rolling single-layer graphene sheets into cylindrical shapes with diameters ranging from 0.4 to 2 nm, which are often arranged in hexagonally close-packed bundles (commonly known as graphene) through SP2 bonding. MWCNTs typically range in length from 0.2 to a few millimeters, with an outer diameter spanning 2 to 100 nm and an inner diameter ranging from one to three nanometers. MWCNTs consist of two or more concentric tubes, each enclosing a hollow core and characterized by a single-layer graphene sheet structure (also known as concentric graphene sheets) [94].

Five strategies commonly employed for CNT synthesis include: a) Laser ablation, b) Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD), c) Silane solution method, d) Flame synthesis technique, and e) Arc Discharge [95,96]. Extensive research has been conducted on CNT-based systems for drug delivery in cancer and antibacterial treatments, as well as their potential applications in photothermal therapy (PTT), photodynamic therapy (PDT), lymph node targeted therapy, and gene manipulation therapy [97,98]. CNTs have shown promise in photothermal tumor and bacterial ablation, selectively eliminating cancer cells and bacteria upon exposure to near-infrared (NIR) light due to their strong optical absorption in the NIR region [99,100]. CNTs' thermal behavior under radiation can be controlled by introducing dopants such as boron or nitrogen, leading to surface defects that enhance their heat conduction ability by scattering light. It is important to consider the length of CNTs, as shorter ones may have limited thermal properties due to poor optical coupling with NIR light. Selecting CNTs of appropriate length can optimize heating efficiency [101].

CNT-based systems have also found applications as tissue engineering scaffolds for antifungal treatment, neurological diseases such as Parkinsonism and Alzheimer's disease, and gene therapy for hereditary diseases [102,103]. Tondroa et al., demonstrated that CNTs@PPy (core@shell configuration of polypyrrole-coated carbon nanotubes) exhibited photothermal conversion capabilities, generating a high level of reactive oxygen species (ROS) that damaged the P. aeruginosa cell membrane, leading to the release of proteins and nucleic acids [62]. Achieving optimal heat during irradiation is a critical challenge to ensure effective treatment, as excessive heating may have adverse effects on nearby healthy tissues, while inadequate heat may result in incomplete destruction of pathological cells.

Graphene is formed by arranging SP2-hybridized carbons into hexagonal rings, resulting in a single graphite sheet. Graphene derivatives contain functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, ketone, and aldehyde groups, among other [104]. Various methods have been developed to produce graphene, including the scotch tape method, carbon nanotube (CNTs) separation, epitaxial growth, colloidal suspension technique, layer-by-layer assembly (LLB), pyrolysis, microwave synthesis, chemical exfoliation, mechanical exfoliation (micromechanical cleavage), solvothermal techniques, chemical vapor deposition (CVD), molecular beam epitaxy, electrically assisted synthesis, and chemical reduction technique [105–108]. The top-down approach involves breaking down pyrolytic graphite or graphene oxide (GO) to create graphene plates. Mechanical exfoliation, chemical synthesis, and chemical exfoliation are three different top-down strategies used to produce graphene sheets from carbon molecules or atoms [108]. Bottom-up methods build graphene sheets from carbon molecules or atoms. These methods can be categorized into four groups: pyrolytic graphene, graphene epitaxial growth on a SiC surface, chemical vapor deposition (CVD), and additional techniques [109,110]. According to reports, graphene has high near-infrared (NIR) absorption qualities and innate heat conductivity, making it the perfect material for photothermal therapy [111,112]. Graphene-based bio-nanocomposites have demonstrated potential for diverse medical applications, including, bone tissue regeneration, osteoporosis, wound healing, anti-tumor and anti-microbial, bioimaging, dental treatments for anti-caries, and pharmaceutical carriers, etc [113–116].(Fig. 4 ).

Numerous research studies have investigated the antibacterial mechanism of graphene and its derivatives. The sharp edges of graphene nanoplates have been found to destroy the cell walls and membranes of bacteria, while also compromising their overall structural integrity and allowing for infiltration by composites [117,118]. It has been observed that GO flakes can envelop, surround, puncture, and ultimately disrupt the cell envelope of bacteria and fungi, leading to electrolyte leakage from fungal spores and disruption of the bacterial cell membrane potential [119]. It has been demonstrated that the size of graphene particles plays a crucial role in their antibacterial effect. Seifi Tahereh et al., employed smaller graphene particles to enhance the dispersion of the material, leading to increased antimicrobial activity through enhanced interactions between the particles and microorganisms [120]. In comparison to conventional bactericidal antibiotics such as kanamycin and streptomycin, rGO/Cu2O exhibited significantly lower Minimum Inhibitory Concentration (MIC) values against Bacillus subtilis, E. coli, and Aeruginosa [121,122]. Akhavan et al., achieved the permanent inactivation of captured live bacteria using graphene oxide sheets via the photothermal effect at a near-infrared wavelength of 808 nm [63]. Furthermore, a multifunctional platform known as TG-NO-B was developed, demonstrating its capability to eradicate multidrug-resistant gram-negative bacteria and their biofilms, as well as promote infected wound healing. This platform exhibited excellent chemotherapeutic and photothermal synergistic antibacterial properties [28]. (Fig. 5
A-E) To enhance the targeted treatment and minimize cellular damage, targeted agents were employed. Anti-Staphylococcus aureus antibodies functionalized with reduced graphene oxide were used to selectively eliminate bacteria. Even at low power densities, the near-infrared irradiation of antibody-rGO complexes resulted in the highly selective destruction of S. aureus [123–126]. In a study by Wu et al., the susceptibility of S. aureus, P. aeruginosa, Candida albicans, and Saccharomyces cerevisiae to antibacterial and antifungal activities was investigated using proline/graphene oxide nano-flakes (GO-Pro/n-Ag). These nanocomposites exhibited a photothermal effect, heating up from 20 °C to 61 °C at a concentration of 100 g/mL upon exposure to near-infrared radiation. Laser irradiation significantly enhanced nanocomposites' antibacterial ability [64]. These studies highlight the diverse applications and effectiveness of graphene-based nanomaterials in combating bacteria and promoting antimicrobial activities. By manipulating the size, functionalization, and combination with other antimicrobial agents, researchers have optimized the antimicrobial properties of graphene materials, opening up new possibilities for their use in antibacterial applications.

To obtain graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), a nitrogen-substituted analog of graphite, it is necessary to thermally polycondense monomers that comprise a conjugated polymer framework. These monomers consist of tris-s-triazine groups connected by tertiary amines, forming a regularly spaced structure [127]. Microwave irradiation can synthesize g-CN using a precursor with a nitrogen-rich C-N backbone [128]. G-C3N4 production is cost-effective as it can be synthesized by thermally treating melamine, dicyandiamide, ammonia, cyanamide, or urea in an atmosphere at temperatures ranging from 480 to 560 °C [129]. Nanomaterials have shown promise for medical applications, as the three-dimensional structure of macroscopic g-C3N4 is reduced to a microscopic two-dimensional structure, leading to an increased quantum yield of emission [130]. The bandgap of g-CN, formed by the interaction of C pz and N pz orbitals, exists between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). This property enables g-CN to be utilized as an antibacterial photocatalytic material, functioning as a semiconductor [131].

C3N4 is a suitable nanomaterial for photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT) due to its ability to undergo water splitting when exposed to high near-infrared (NIR) or highly penetrating red light (greater than600 nm). Nanomaterials with red-shifted band gaps are specifically designed for PDT/PTT applications to split water upon exposure to red or NIR light, resulting in the generation of oxygen molecules. This synthesis approach aims to combat hypoxia during PDT [132]. Wang et al., conducted the synthesis of nanocomposites comprising Prussian blue nanoparticles (PBNPs) and graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets (g-C3N4-H+ nanosheets) through electrostatic interactions [65]. The obtained results demonstrated that these nanocomposites exhibited enhanced photothermal properties and displayed superior synergistic therapeutic effects in photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) for cancer treatment when compared to the standalone g-C3N4-H+ nanosheets system. In the study by Zhang et al., a magnetic chitosan-graphene surface coated with vancomycin was found to effectively inhibit the growth of methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) upon NIR exposure, providing an efficient antibacterial solution [66]. Similarly, graphene oxide functionalized with glutaraldehyde (MRGOGA) exhibited rapid bactericidal activity against both gram-positive S. aureus and gram-negative E. coli bacteria when irradiated with NIR lasers [67]. Qian et al., reported that glycolic chitosan functionalized with carboxyl graphene (GCS-CG), when placed at the sites of acidic focal infections or abscesses, can harness the photothermal effect to selectively eliminate focal infection bacteria while minimally affecting normal cells [68]. These studies demonstrate the potential of nanomaterials, such as C3N4, magnetic chitosan-graphene, and graphene oxide, in PDT/PTT applications for antibacterial purposes. By utilizing specific light wavelengths and functionalizing nanomaterials, researchers have achieved effective antibacterial solutions with minimal impact on healthy cells, opening up new avenues for targeted and selective antibacterial treatments.

Noble metal photothermal agents (PTAs), including gold (Au), silver (Ag), platinum (Pt), ruthenium (Ru), and palladium (Pd), possess unique photophysical properties. These materials are typically considered inert. They exhibit strong surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effects, large absorbance cross-sections, tunable aspect ratios, and high photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE) in the near-infrared (NIR) region [133–135]. He et al., synthesized two-dimensional AuPd alloy nanosheets with a thickness of approximately 1.5 nm using these noble metals [136]. (Fig. 6
A-D) The nanosheets demonstrated high photothermal conversion efficiency (η = 76.6%), biocompatibility, stable optical signal, and excellent generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). When noble metal nanomaterials absorb light energy at a specific wavelength resonant with their absorption properties, the free electrons within these materials resonate around the surface of the nanoparticle [137]. This unique photophysical phenomenon, known as local surface plasmon resonance (LSPR), enables noble metal nanomaterials to undergo photothermal conversion, making them highly suitable for photothermal antimicrobial applications [138,139]. The LSPR band in the NIR region and the heat generation capacity can be influenced by the surface charge density, which is determined by the size, shape, structure, dielectric properties, morphology, and inter-particle coupling of noble metal nanomaterials [140,141]. Noble metal nanoparticles, such as gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), have been extensively studied for their antibacterial properties, including their ability to combat drug-resistant bacteria and even induce antibiotic susceptibility in resistant strains [142,143]. By manipulating the form, size, and nanostructures of noble metal materials, it is possible to adjust their photothermal and antibacterial activities. However, noble metal nanoparticle production can be challenging and costly. Common precious metal nanomaterials have been identified as broad-spectrum antimicrobial agents that function via photothermal mechanisms. However, current research in this area has been limited to a few bacterial strains [144]. Several studies have investigated the photothermal antibacterial properties of precious metal nanomaterials, with a focus on their efficacy against various bacterial strains. Notably, gold nanomaterials have been shown to effectively eliminate bacteria such as Gram-negative (P. aeruginosa, E. faecalis, and E. coli) and Gram-positive (S. aureus and S. epidermidis) bacteria, as well as drug-resistant bacteria (vancomycin-resistant enterococci (MRSA) [145–147]. Silver nanomaterials have been shown to effectively eliminate various bacterial strains through photothermal mechanisms, including E. coli and S. aureus, methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus and beta-lactamase-producing E. coli strains [148,149]. Similarly, palladium nanomaterials have been found to exhibit photothermal antibacterial activity against certain bacterial strains, such as S. enterococcus faecalis, also including S. aureus, E. coli, C. albicans, B. cereus, and candida utilize [144,150]. Photothermal antimicrobial performance of platinum nanomaterials against E. coli and MRSA [151]. A summary of the antibacterial properties of various types of noble metal nanomaterials is shown in Table 2 .

Gold exhibits chemically inert properties, high ductility, and excellent adjustability in terms of size, shape, structure, and morphology. It also possesses remarkable resistance to corrosion and oxidation. In the field of photothermal therapy (PTT), gold nanomaterials have garnered considerable attention due to their exceptional light absorption, stability, adjustable optical properties, strong biocompatibility, and efficient heating effect resulting from the abundance of free electrons on their surfaces [169]. Various types of gold nanomaterials with efficient near-infrared (NIR) absorption properties have been synthesized, including nanorods, nanospheres, nanostars, nanocages, and nanoshells [144,170]. These anisotropic gold nanomaterials generate higher heat energy and exhibit a wider range of localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effects, thereby enhancing the photothermal conversion effect [171]. In addition to the aforementioned nanostructures, other gold nanostructures such as gold nanospheres, gold nanoshells, gold nanoparticles (GNPs), and gold nanocages have been employed for PTT in the treatment of bacterial infections [172]. Well-defined gold nanoparticles with specific compositions can be synthesized using physical, chemical, and biological methods [173]. In an experiment involving spherical GNPs, it was observed that when coupled with Staphylococcus aureus and subjected to high-intensity laser irradiation, only the coupled bacteria were affected, while the uncoupled bacteria remained unaffected. Antibody-functionalized GNPs specifically bind to bacteria and mediate the photothermal effect, resulting in antibacterial activity [174].

Gold nanoparticles (GNPs) offer a versatile platform with multiple functionalities, including hyperthermia. Previous studies have conjugated vancomycin with GNPs to enhance vancomycin sensitivity against vancomycin-resistant enterococci [175]. Huang et al., demonstrated that vancomycin conjugated to GNPs induces photothermal disruption of bacteria through surface proton local resonance, with photodynamic therapy (PDT) also playing a role in the process [175]. This binding effect exhibits mutual reinforcement [176]. Kuo et al., conducted experimental studies to evaluate GNPs' effectiveness as antibacterial agents when conjugated with various antibiotics. The results showed that GNP conjugates were more effective at inhibiting the growth and killing of both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria than antibiotics alone [177]. Next, the researchers conducted some experimental studies to determine how effective GNPs are as antibacterial agents when conjugated with a variety of antibiotics, and they concluded that GNP conjugates were more effective in inhibiting growth and killing Gram (both Gram-positives and Gram-negatives) than the same antibiotics alone [145,146]. In addition to their antimicrobial properties, gold nanomaterials also promote collagen synthesis and facilitate granulation tissue. This speeds wound healing and trauma recovery. When combined with other molecules such as Prussian blue (PB), black phosphorus (BP), red phosphorus (RB), indocyanine green (ICG), heptamethine cyanines (e.g., IR780), or other materials like graphene oxide (rGO), silica, and iron oxide, gold nanomaterials exhibit superior photothermal conversion efficiency [1,178–180]. These findings highlight the potential of GNPs for various applications, including enhancing antibiotic efficacy, inducing photothermal disruption of bacteria, and promoting tissue regeneration.

Gold nanorods with tunable aspect ratios have been synthesized using a simple photochemical process. The aspect ratio, which refers to the ratio of length to diameter, of the resulting gold nanorods (GNRs) is determined by the amount of silver ions used in the synthesis process, with the silver ions subsequently being separated from the GNRs [181]. By adjusting the aspect ratio, it is possible to tune the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) region of the GNRs and shift it towards the near-infrared (NIR) region, which is desirable for photothermal therapy (PTT) [24]. Small-sized GNRs possess a large absorbance cross-section and a narrow absorbance spectrum, making them suitable for PTT applications [182–184]. GNRs with specific aspect ratios exhibit remarkable transmembrane transport and diffusion rates, allowing them to accumulate in focal infection tissues and penetrate focal infection cells more rapidly than other gold nanomaterials [185]. Norman et al., targeted nanorods to Pseudomonas aeruginosa isolates (PA3) cells, resulting in extensive bacterial cell death and membrane rupture. Photothermal therapy of persistent in vivo pathogenic bacterial biofilms or infections was achieved by exposing P. aeruginosa cells to a NIR laser in the presence of a conjugate of PA3 antibody and nanorods. The bacterial-specific antibody, through surface functionalization, binds to bacteria and produces antibacterial effects [152]. Liu et al., utilized polydopamine (PDA) coating-encapsulated GNRs to create a chemo-photothermal therapeutic platform (Ag+-GCS-PDA@GNRs) functionalized with silver ions and GCS [153]. This antimicrobial platform exhibits targeting aggregation properties and enables synergistic chemiluminescence-photothermal therapy, effectively destroying bacteria, accelerating wound healing, and minimizing damage to normal tissue. (Fig. 7 ).

Gold nanorods (GNRs) can be functionalized using a disulfide bond–connected poly (ethylene glycol)-b-poly(ε-caprolactone) block copolymer, enabling dense wrapping of GNRs with controlled interparticle direction of movement. This functionalization strategy further enhances photothermal therapy (PTT) by enabling localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) in the near-infrared (NIR) window [186]. In the context of photothermal antibacterial agents, poly(acrylic acid) (PAAc) chains on silicone rubber (SR) films serve as a site for efficient gold ion collection, facilitating the reduction reaction to produce gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) [187]. Moreover, modification of GNRs with Buforin 2b (BF2b) peptide results in strong affinity for methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). These peptide-modified GNRs exhibit reduced cytotoxicity and significantly enhanced bactericidal activity upon NIR light exposure. In vitro and in vivo studies demonstrate improved antibacterial efficacy of modified GNRs [154]. Additionally, the surface of Ti-GNRs exhibits superior antibacterial activity against Gram-negative bacteria (such as Escherichia coli and Pseudomonas aeruginosa) compared to Gram-positive bacteria (such as Staphylococcus aureus and Staphylococcus epidermidis) in the presence of NIR irradiation. This observation is attributed to the increased contact area and relatively fragile cell wall structure of Gram-negative bacteria under these conditions [147]. In recent studies, selective nanorods (MoS2-AuNRs-apt) have been synthesized for the investigation of Gram-negative bacterial infections, which have shown increasing resistance to conventional antibiotic therapies [155]. These nanorods were prepared using a combination of molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) nanosheets, gold nanorods (AuNRs), and lipopolysaccharide (LPS). The specific targeting of LPS, guided by an 808 nm laser, facilitated both anti-inflammatory and photothermal treatments specifically tailored for multidrug-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (MRPA) infections [155]. These findings highlight the potential of functionalized GNRs in PTT, with controlled interparticle movement, LSPR in the NIR window, and enhanced antibacterial efficacy against MRSA. The differential response of Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria to Ti-GNRs by NIR irradiation. Valuable insights into the development of nanomaterial-based strategies for combating antibiotic-resistant bacterial infections, particularly Gram-negative bacteria.

Through the conjugation of specific materials or molecules, gold nanospheres exhibit the ability to selectively target specific areas, enhance nonlinear optical absorption, and improve laser utilization. These properties result in the induction of two-photon absorption and second harmonic production, both contributing to cellular death [188]. Moreover, in colloid solutions, gold nanospheres (GNSPs) tend to undergo electrostatic clustering, further augmenting near-infrared (NIR) laser irradiation [189]. Yuan et al., conducted a study demonstrating that cation-coupled ions facilitate the formation of aggregates with gold nanospheres in solution. Notably, these aggregates demonstrate a high affinity toward pathogenic bacteria, exhibit low toxicity against mammalian cells, and effectively treat multidrug-resistant bacterial skin infections [156].

In photothermal applications, hollow gold nanospheres (HGNs) offer advantages over solid gold nanospheres (GNSPs) due to their enhanced absorption efficiency in the near-infrared (NIR) region. Xie et al., investigated the derivatization of GNSPs and successfully synthesized HGNs by incorporating inorganic nanoparticles such as cobalt (Co) and silica [190]. By precisely controlling their localized surface plasmon resonances, HGNs can extend their absorption properties to wavelengths as long as 1320 nm [190]. To compare the absorption efficiencies of various nanoparticle solutions, including gold, silver, and HGNs, an irradiation process utilizing an excitable light source at a wavelength of 785 nm was performed for a duration of 45 min [190]. The results revealed that HGNs exhibited the highest absorption efficiency among the tested nanoparticles [190]. HGNs' superior absorption efficiency in the NIR range makes them a promising candidate for photothermal applications. Through the incorporation of inorganic nanoparticles and precise tuning of their localized surface plasmon resonances, HGNs can extend their absorption capabilities to longer wavelengths, thereby maximizing their photothermal conversion efficiency.

Gold nanostars (AuNSs) possess a small spherical gold core with numerous sharp projecting gold tips extending from it [191]. AuNSs generate plasmons from the intricate interaction between the core and the tips. Specifically, the core acts as a receiver, significantly enhancing the excitation cross-sections and improving the confinement of the electric field produced by the tip plasmons [191]. IThis interaction ultimately generates a robust heat flow at the interface between the metal and the dielectric by transferring the oscillating surface electrons' energy to the atomic lattice [192]. In terms of promoting biodistribution for effective heating, smaller AuNSs in the size range of 25–50 nm demonstrate superior performance. However, it is critical to note that prolonged exposure to AuNSs under near-infrared (NIR) lasers can potentially harm normal tissue, necessitating careful consideration and avoidance [193]. The commonly employed method for synthesizing AuNSs is the seed-mediated technique [194].

Gold nanostars (AuNSs) can be synthesized through the reduction of gold compounds, such as chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), in the presence of surfactants like cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) and poly(N-vinylpyrrolidone) [195]. This synthetic process leads to the formation of well-defined AuNSs with distinctive optical properties encompassing the tips and central core [196]. The number of tips and the development of branched structures in gold nanoparticles can be controlled by incorporating piperazine in the particle synthesis using 2-[4-(2-hydroxyethyl) piperazin-1-yl] ethane sulfonic acid (HEPES) [197]. Furthermore, the utilization of copper (Cu) as a reducing agent in oleylamine can result in the successful synthesis of cross-shaped AuNSs, with the size of the obtained gold nano-crosses varying depending on the Cu species present [197]. Introducing silver nitrate into the synthesis process can also modulate the morphology of AuNSs [195]. Espinosa et al., demonstrated that the redshift of the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) peak in gold nanostars is positively correlated with the core size, the number and length of the tips, as well as their sharpness. (Fig. 8
A) It is evident that the heat generated by light increases accordingly with the redshift of the LSPR peak, indicating enhanced photothermal conversion efficiency [198]. Grisoli et al., investigated films composed of mixed silver nanoparticles (AgNP) and gold nanostars (GNS) to demonstrate their ability to enhance the antibacterial effect and prolong the duration of action [199]. ( Fig. 8 B-D) In the study by Espinosa et al., thin films of a PDMS-GNSs hybrid, comprising polydimethylsiloxane and gold nanostars with LSPR absorption capacity, exhibited photothermal-based bactericidal effects upon NIR irradiation, showing promise for their potential use in prosthetic implants [157]. Borzenkov et al., discovered that NIR-irradiated biocompatible films composed of poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) containing poly(ethylene glycol) (PEGylated) gold nanostars displayed a significant photothermal effect, effectively eliminating bacteria on the film surface. GNS optimization was explored to achieve a more efficient method for eradicating bacteria and bacterial biofilms using low laser power [158]. (Fig. 8 E-F).

Gold nanocages (AuNCs) exhibit unique quasi-cubic shapes with extremely thin walls, featuring a hollow interior and a porous exterior [200]. These plasmonic nanoparticles possess remarkable adjustable optical characteristics, making them highly suitable for various biological applications, including photothermal therapy [201,202]. AuNCs are synthesized by a galvanic replacement reaction between silver nanocubes and chloroauric acid (HAuCl4)/Au(III) ions, where silver ions diffuse into the solution due to the difference in electrochemical potentials between the two materials. Concurrently, a thin layer of Au(0) is epitaxially deposited on the outer surface of silver nanocubes [203]. The ongoing progress of this reaction leads to the elimination of the gold-silver walls and the internal silver of the nanocubes, resulting in the formation of hollow and porous structures, namely nanocages [204]. To create silver nanocubes, a reduction process involving ethylene glycol, polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), and a small quantity of sodium sulfide is employed. These silver nanocubes then serve as templates in the galvanic replacement process, where gold nanocages are synthesized [205]. The size and wall thickness of the gold nanocages can be easily and precisely adjusted, allowing for tunable absorbance or absorption at specific wavelengths in the near-infrared (NIR) spectrum, which is a critical property of gold nanomaterials [206]. The thickness of the gold layer is determined by the quantity of gold precursor (e.g., HAuCl4) mixed into the solution [207]. By manipulating the ratio of HAuCl4 to silver and the Ag/Au ratio, the gold layer thickness can be modified to shift the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) peaks of the gold nanocages towards the near-infrared region. The resulting gold nanocages exhibited an LSPR peak at around 800 nm by adjusting the silver-to-HAuCl4 ratios [208]. Yang et al., demonstrated that increasing the amount of HAuCl4 solution added to silver nanocubes caused the LSPR peak to shift from 600 nm to 1200 nm [209,210]. These AuNCs displayed a substantial absorption cross-sectional area of 3.48 × 10–14 m2 and possessed a compact size suitable for in vivo distribution, enabling efficient conversion of near-infrared (NIR) radiation into thermal energy. Notably, the absorption cross-sectional area was five orders of magnitude larger than that of indocyanine green, highlighting AuNCs' superior photothermal properties [204,211].

In a study by Meeker et al., a synergistic photothermal and antibiotic approach was employed to achieve rapid bacterial killing and reduce bacterial resistance [159]. Polydopamine-coated gold nanocages (AuNC@PDA) were synthesized and combined with the effective antibacterial Daptomycin. AuNC@PDA could be conjugated with antibodies targeting a specific surface protein, staphylococcal protein A (Spa), enabling selective binding to the surface of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and generating heat upon exposure to near-infrared (NIR) light. After photothermal heating, daptomycin is released, resulting in a synergistic antibiotic-photothermal effect that effectively eliminates S. aureus. This combined approach exhibited the capability to rapidly kill bacteria through photothermal action, which occurs immediately after radiation-induced bactericidal activity, as well as to diminish bacterial viability and antibiotic resistance effects, which occur within 24 h [159]. Wu et al., synthesized Au-Ag@SiO2 nanocages (NCs) that demonstrated excellent in vivo anti-infective capabilities due to the combined functions of NIR radiation-mediated heating and sustained release of silver (Ag) ions. These NCs possessed hollow interiors and extremely thin porous outer walls, making them potential carriers of antibiotics [160]. Another study demonstrated that gold nanocages coated with macrophage membranes could effectively destroy bacteria upon attachment to bacterial cell walls via macrophage receptors. Laser irradiation significantly reduced bacterial counts both in vitro and in vivo, as the nanocages converted laser light into heat, while the macrophage membranes specifically recognized bacteria. Furthermore, the unique structure of the nanocages, with hollow interiors and porous walls, allowed for loading of antibacterial drugs for controlled release upon NIR light stimulation [212]. These findings highlight the potential for synergistic photothermal and antibiotic approaches using gold nanocages. They also highlight their applications in rapid bacterial killing, reduction of bacterial resistance, and controlled drug release.

In the production process of Gold Nanoshells (AuNSs), an amine-terminated surface silanizing agent, such as 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APTMS), is applied to the core of silica nanoparticles [213,214]. This agent serves as an attachment site for the preparation process, particularly for relatively small gold seeds, approximately 2 nm in size [213]. The synthesis involves the reduction of chloroauric acid and tetrakis(hydroxymethyl)phosphonium chloride (THPC) to generate tiny gold nanoparticles with a diameter of 2 nm [215,216]. hrough coordination with the lone pairs of the terminal amine groups, this method leads to the formation of silica nanoparticles with approximately 25% of their surface covered with small colloidal gold nanoparticles [217,218]. The entire production process entails the preparation and functionalization of silica nanoparticles, synthesis of very small gold nanoparticles (2 nm in diameter), attachment of these seeds to the silica core, and the subsequent synthesis of shells in a solution commonly referred to as “K gold” [215,219]. Gao et al., demonstrated the creation of multilayered gold nanoshells with viable plasmonic controllability and distinctive multi-layered core–shell nanoparticle structures by utilizing gold-thiol interactions. This was achieved by adhering tiny (2 nm) gold nanoparticles onto the surface of silica shell-gold core nanostructures [220].

The rapid manufacture of gold seeds with a narrow particle size distribution (2–3 nm diameter range) was developed by Brito-Silva et al., and can be completed within 15 min. The method utilizes polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) with an average molecular weight of 55,000 as a stabilizer, along with sodium borohydride (NaBH4) as a reducer [219]. Gold nanoshells were functionalized with carboxylate-terminated organosulfur compounds and then attached to silicone substrates covered in polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS). This approach provided a near-infrared (NIR)-induced method for eliminating Enterococcus faecalis (E. faecalis) bacteria attached to the nanoshells [161]. Oh et al., developed an efficient bactericidal approach for photothermal destruction of antibiotic-resistant bacteria, including Gram-negative (Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Escherichia coli) and Gram-positive (Staphylococcus aureus) bacteria, using thiolated chitosan-wrapped gold nanoshells. These nanoshells exhibited excellent absorption properties for NIR irradiation and efficient photothermal conversion capacity [162]. The combination of gold nanoshells with NIR irradiation was highly effective in rapidly eliminating bacteria and inhibiting their regeneration, leveraging the antibacterial capabilities of the nanoshells and their photothermal conversion capacity [162]. After 5 min of NIR irradiation exposure, all bacteria were eliminated, and no new bacteria were formed even after 48 h of NIR exposure [163]. Irradiating E. coli with gold nanoshells at a wavelength of 650 nm demonstrated a more effective sterilizing effect than treating the bacteria with gold nanoshells alone, highlighting the advantages of this combined approach [163]. Loo et al., demonstrated that reducing the thickness of gold nanoshells at a specific inner core diameter led to a red shift in the absorption peak of the nanoshells [221]. (Fig. 9
A-B) NIR light exposure increased drug release to 92% and 69% at specific pH levels, while without NIR irradiation, these rates decreased to 80% and 55%, respectively. This is attributed to gold nanoshells' ability to absorb light in the NIR spectrum, triggering controlled drug release [222]. Recent studies have shown that polyethylene glycol-thiol (PEG-SH) and l-cysteine can be used to chemically bind antimicrobial properties to the hollowed gold shell, which features a spherical core–shell structure enabling efficient regulation of drug release and distribution by AuNSs. (Fig. 9 C) Additionally, immunoglobulin G (IgG) can be coupled to l-cysteine carboxyl for targeted drug administration, resulting in significantly increased sterilizing power [223]. (Fig. 9 D).

Gold nanoplates (AuNPLs) possess distinct advantages over their 0D and 1D counterparts as they exhibit intense near-infrared (NIR) surface plasmon resonance (SPR) peaks, large surface areas, sharp edges, and angles, enabling a wide range of applications [171,224]. Recent studies have employed various synthetic strategies to achieve high-yield production of AuNPLs with an anisotropic nanostructure characterized by a significantly larger lateral dimension than their thickness [171,225]. The choice of capping agents plays a crucial role in AuNPL synthesis as they selectively adsorb onto specific gold surfaces, hindering gold ions adsorption on these surfaces and confining the growth to a particular plane. Polymers, biomolecules, surfactants such as cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), and halide ions are among the commonly used capping agents [226–228]. CTAB, in particular, forms a strong complex with halides, making it an efficient surfactant for AuNPL synthesis, as it readily adsorbs onto gold surfaces. To prepare single-crystal AuNPLs with a lateral dimension in the micron range and a 60% yield, a reaction temperature of 85 °C and a CTAB/Au molar ratio of 6:1 are required [229]. The Millstone group has developed a method utilizing gold nanoparticles and CTAB as a capping agent, enabling the synthesis of small AuNPLs [230]. Xin et al., found that Br- ions promote nanoplate morphologies, while Cl- ions accelerate gold deposition and inhibit anisotropic growth [229]. Multilayer graphene sheets, together with two gold precursors (KAuBr4 and HAuCl4), can be employed to adjust the lateral dimension of AuNPLs ranging from nanometers to micrometers [231]. Some studies have demonstrated that Br- ions can act as surfactants in a straightforward one-pot technique to synthesize large 2D AuNPLs on carbon nanotube (CNT) sheets [232]. Comparatively, the biofunctionalization of AuNPLs directed at polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) is relatively straightforward compared to halide-directed synthesis. Yun et al., have shown that by utilizing PVP as a directing agent instead of CTAB, single-crystalline nanoplates with controlled size and thickness can be achieved by adjusting the molar ratio between PVP and HAuCl4 [233].

AuNPLs (gold nanosheets) with adjustable thickness, lateral dimensions, and plasmonic properties have demonstrated remarkable capability in inducing photothermal cell ablation under the near-infrared (NIR) I and NIR II biological windows, surpassing the performance of most reported gold-based nanoreagents, particularly under NIR II irradiation [164]. These AuNPLs exhibit exceptional photothermal properties, characterized by a significant photothermal conversion efficiency of 68.5%. Moreover, they possess peroxidase-like activity. In a recent study conducted by Yan et al., synergistic antibacterial experiments were carried out using AuNPTs (gold nanoprism)/H2O2/NIR against methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) and Escherichia coli K12 [164]. This approach involved the utilization of low doses of AuNPLs in combination with exceedingly low concentrations of H2O2 under 808 nm laser irradiation. The experimental results demonstrated that the AuNPTs/H2O2/NIR treatment achieved 99.98% inactivation of both MRSA and E. coli K12 within an exceptionally short timeframe of 3 min, surpassing the duration required for antibacterial activity solely through photothermal means.

By using silver materials with different shapes as the initial templates, a wide variety of other structures can further be synthesized, including gold nanorings, aggregated AuNPs, prism-shaped nano boxes, polygonal AuNPs, Au nanotubes, gold anoprisms, gold nanoflowers, gold caterpillar-like nanoparticles, and gold nanoclusters [139,194,234–237]. In a recent investigation focusing on Au nanoclusters, a notable advancement was achieved through the synthesis of Au25Capt18 hydrogels [165]. These hydrogels were prepared by cross-linking carrageenan subsequent to the synthesis of gold nanoclusters (Au25Capt18) encapsulated with captopril, using an alkaline NaBH4 reduction method. This composite hydrogel exhibited activated photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT), with PTT playing a more significant role in antimicrobial efficacy than PDT. The hydrogel, when subjected to 808 nm laser irradiation at a power density of 2.0 W/cm2, demonstrated a remarkable pathogenic bacteria clearance rate exceeding 90% and facilitated the wound healing process in bacterial infections. This research offers novel insights into the development of hydrogel-based systems combined with photothermal nanomaterials, thereby paving the way for advancements in the field of biomedical materials for therapeutic applications [238].

Silver nanoparticles, also known as nanocrystalline silver, have demonstrated significant clinical advancements in various applications, including antimicrobial therapy, diagnostics, tissue regeneration, and antimicrobial agents in nanomaterials [239]. Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) with antibiotic properties in medical products such as wound dressings and catheters have been implemented in vivo and in vitro, with some undergoing clinical trials [240]. Several factors have contributed to the success of silver nanoparticles in precision medicine, including their favorable biocompatibility, ease of manufacturing and integration, metabolic characteristics, antimicrobial properties, tunable SPR bands, and efficient photothermal conversion capabilities [241,242].

The integration of metal ions with antimicrobial properties into a nano-system of polydopamine (PDA) under near-infrared (NIR) radiation enhances PDA's antimicrobial efficacy [243]. Combining photothermal nanomaterials with silver coatings in nanocomposites enables the simultaneous release of silver ions and the generation of high heat effects, resulting in a mutually reinforcing effect [244]. In recent advancements, a groundbreaking photothermal antimicrobial platform was developed by incorporating Ag nanocrystal-decorated polydopamine (PDA) nanoparticles. This innovative design aimed to significantly improve the photothermal conversion efficiency, antibacterial effectiveness, and wound healing capabilities of the platform [244]. (Fig. 10
E) For example, by assembling graphene-based nanosheets (GBNs) with silver nanoparticles, the photothermal effect induced by GBNs stimulates the direct interaction of silver ions with bacterial proteins, leading to protein denaturation. This highly effective sterilization approach involves the destruction of bacterial membranes, facilitating the entry of more silver nanoparticles into the bacterial interior [245,246]. Furthermore, the release of Ag+ ions from glycol chitosan (GCS) and their grafting onto polydopamine-coated gold nanorods (GNRs), referred to as Ag+-GCS-PDA@GNRs, is significantly enhanced by hyperthermia induced by NIR light irradiation. In a notable study, Liu et al., intravenously administered f-Ag+-GCS-PDA@GNRs (4.7 mg/kg) to mice with abdominal abscesses, demonstrating the platform's exceptional accumulation capacity and robust thermotherapeutic ability at the abscess site, while exhibiting minimal fluorescence activation in normal skin tissue [153]. (Fig. 10 A-C) Alternatively, a combination of photothermal therapy (PTT) and other antibacterial agents can be employed for effective bacteria treatment [247]. Under laser irradiation, the presence of antimicrobial or noble metal ions, such as silver ions, promotes the release of reactive oxidative species (ROS), alters bacterial heat sensitivity, induces membrane damage, and physically disrupts bacterial membranes and DNA. Impairment of DNA replication capability ultimately affects bacterial survival [247]. In addition to gold (Au) nanocomposites, plasmonic silver ion-related nano complexes have been demonstrated to exert a substantial influence on bacterial growth and biofilm stability under near-infrared (NIR) laser radiation, particularly for clinically relevant strains such as Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) [149]. Xiong et al., investigated the antimicrobial efficacy of silver sulfide (Ag2S) nanoparticle-loaded nano-cubes (Ag2S/NCs) derived from metal-organic frameworks when subjected to laser light at 808 nm, revealing excellent antimicrobial action against S. aureus [166]. (Fig. 10 D-F) It has been established that spherical silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) with surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effects in photothermal therapy (PTT) do not penetrate deep into tissues. However, anisotropic AgNPs can be engineered to precisely tune their SPR to the NIR region, including silver nanotriangles, silver nanospheres, and nanocages, thereby enabling enhanced tissue penetration capabilities [171,248,249]. Chen et al., conducted a study exploring the impact of silver nanoparticles of various sizes on the antimicrobial activities of GO@PEG@AgNPs [167]. Notably, samples containing the smallest silver nanoparticles exhibited the highest antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus aureus [167]. The presence of more number of active silver atoms in smaller particles facilitates the generation of reactive oxygen species, resulting in bactericidal effects [167]. These nanoparticles exhibit a high loading capacity on graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets, leading to the disruption of bacterial cell membranes and subsequent release of silver nanoparticles into bacterial cells, thereby enhancing their antimicrobial efficiency [149,167].

Moreover, the shape of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) plays a crucial role in enhanced antimicrobial activity, in addition to the well-established negative correlation between AgNP size and antimicrobial efficacy, as elucidated by Pal et al., [250]. Truncated triangular silver nanoplates possess the strongest antimicrobial properties, followed by silver nanospheres and silver nanorods. It is observed that various shapes of AgNPs interact with cell membranes, leading to destruction. Notably, the surfaces of truncated triangular silver nanoplates exhibit enhanced biocidal activity compared to the latter two shapes. This unique feature may contribute to improved surface adherence, cellular absorption, and efficient bacteria eradication [250]. Franci et al., have further demonstrated that triangular silver nanoplates (Tri-Ag) possess an atom-rich crystal structure, which induces alterations in the permeability of bacterial biofilms, provokes morphological changes in the bacterial cell membrane, disrupts bacterial respiration, and ultimately exhibits remarkable antibacterial activity upon adherence to the bacterial surface [251]. Triangular silver nanoplates (Tri-Ag) exhibit immense potential in NIR laser-assisted combination therapy for combating bacterial infections. In their groundbreaking research, Qiao et al., successfully synthesized Tri-Ag nanoparticles with an approximate diameter of 60 nm and a thickness of around 10 nm, employing a facile one-step seedless artificial technique. Notably, the seedless nature of this technique enables the adjustment of surface plasmon resonance (SPR) properties, encompassing a broad spectrum from the visible to the NIR region, owing to manipulable experimental parameters [251,252]. In Qiao et al.’s experiments, they found that group Spherical silver nanoparticles (Sph-Ag) had a very low bactericidal rate (24.2% E. coli and 26.1% for S. aureus) while group B killed more than 90% of the bacteria when exposed to the same laser infrared laser radiation. As a result of using the same concentration of penicillin for both groups, the group using A under laser inhibited bacterial growth much more effectively than the group using penicillin alone [252]. The photothermal conversion rate of 58.67 %, measured by UV/vis spectrometer, of Tri-Ag, was strongly correlated with its strong absorption in the NIR region, which is critical for achieving high photothermal efficiency [148]. Remarkably, the utilization of Tri-Ag in conjunction with NIR laser irradiation has demonstrated augmented antibacterial efficacy against multidrug-resistant bacteria strains, notably including methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) and beta-lactamase-producing E. coli strains, highlighting the immense potential of this combined therapeutic approach [148].

Due to its cost-effectiveness and high efficiency in photothermal therapy, palladium (Pd) has emerged as a promising candidate for photothermal agents (PTAs) owing to its exceptional heat resistance and stability in photothermal conversion [253]. Moreover, Pd exhibits resistance to high-temperature deformation caused by near-infrared radiation [254]. Pd nanoparticles (PdNPs) and palladium nanosheets (PdNSs) have been utilized in antibacterial applications and photothermal therapy. Several studies have demonstrated the antibacterial efficacy of PdNPs against Enterococcus faecalis, Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, Candida albicans, Bacillus cereus, and Candida species [255,256]. Adams et al., attributed the higher antimicrobial activity of Pd nanoparticles to their small diameters, which make them effective antimicrobial agents against S. aureus compared to E. coli [150]. Various efficient, environmentally friendly, cost-effective, reliable, and reusable synthesis methods are available for PdNPs. Anand et al., utilized a reduction reaction to produce PdNPs from agroforestry wastes and Moringa oleifera flower extracts, which exhibited significant cytotoxicity against E. faecalis as evidenced by the inhibition zone of bacterial growth, while not inducing toxicity in healthy peripheral lymphocytes [257]. Gurunathan et al., described the utilization of evolvulus alsinoides leaf extracts in the production of PdNPs without the need for additional reduction or stabilizing reactions [258].

To increase the number of bacteria entering, Tang et al., increased the thickness of the PdNSs, which affects bacterial internalization [259]. Through silica coating, PdNSs are altered, resulting in an increase in thickness and a positive charge induced by surface functionalization. Functionalized silica-coated Pd nanosheets were significantly more effective at killing bacteria with NIR photothermal conversion energy when endocytosed by bacteria over Pd nanosheets. The smaller size of PdNSs offers several advantages, including improved renal clearance and reduced toxicity, making them highly suitable for practical applications in Photothermal Therapy (PTT) for inpatient treatments [260]. In the NIR wavelength range between 600 and 800 nm, Zhao et al., employed a series of single continuous wave (CW) lasers operating at 660 nm to simultaneously induce potent Photodynamic Therapy (PDT) and PTT effects [261]. This was accomplished by synthesizing nanocomposites of chlorin e6 (Ce6)-functionalized Pd nanosheets (Pd-PEI-Ce6) as a photosensitizer, where Polyethyleneimine (PEI) served as the binder for Ce6 and Pd nanosheets, which functioned as both PDT and PTT agents. Additionally, He et al., utilized peptide-modified Gold-Palladium (AuPd) nanosheets under NIR irradiation to achieve precise PTT and PDT of Staphylococcus aureus-infected lesions guided by photoacoustic imaging (PAI-guided), thereby promoting effective wound healing [136]. ( Fig. 11
A-K).

Photothermal therapy (PTT) employs platinum nanoparticles (Pt NPs) to generate a controlled and moderate heating effect, resulting in modest hyperthermia that does not exceed 46 °C. This temperature range induces apoptosis while preserving healthy cells from damage [262]. The secure bonding of Pt2+ ions to the surface of the nanoparticles prevents unwanted drug leakage and non-selective harm to healthy tissues, as it avoids exposure to light. Additionally, Pt NPs offer controlled release, resistance to light deactivation, superior physicochemical properties, and photothermal stability [262]. Various synthetic approaches have been investigated for the fabrication of elemental Pt nanoparticles, including ultrathin Pt nanowires and nanosheets [263,264]. These approaches encompass hydrothermal and solvothermal methods, electrochemical deposition techniques, sol–gel processes, electroless deposition techniques, and physical synthesis methods [265,266]. However, clinically approved stabilizers and organic ligands lack in these synthetic approaches. Commonly employed substances for assisting nucleation, subsequent synthesis, isotropic or anisotropic growth, and post-modification of Pt nanoparticles include polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP), citric acid, ethylenediamine, dendrimers, and mesoporous silica [180,265,267]. In another study, the ratio of PtCl4-2 to poly(amidoamine) (PAMAM-C12) dendrimers, along with the use of alkyl chain-terminated dendrimers as the foundation, enabled facile control over PtNPs synthesis [268]. Physicochemical experiments involving the dissolution of platinum(II) acetylacetonate in oleylamine and octadecene led to the synthesis of Pt nanoworms. These Pt nano worms exhibited high photothermal efficiency, enabling mild PTT within the second near-infrared (NIR) window [269]. Moreover, the nano worms demonstrated consistent shape and physicochemical stability under physiological conditions, and there were no noticeable adverse effects associated with PTT at moderate laser power without overheating [194,269]. In recent studies, Pt spirals have been identified as promising nanomaterials due to their high extinction coefficients and broad surface plasmon resonance (SPR) range spanning over 1000 nm. This unique property makes them highly effective in photothermal applications, especially in the near-infrared (NIR) region. The Pt spirals demonstrate a remarkable photothermal conversion rate of approximately 52.5%, enabling deep tissue penetration and efficient thermal ablation. Pt spirals were acceptable cytotoxicity without the death of mice through in vivo studies [168].
