Deng et al., have successfully synthesized bivalent platinum nanoparticles (dvPtNPs) for photothermal antibacterial studies [151]. The dvPtNPs consist of zero-valent Pt cores (Pt0) and divalent Pt shells (Pt2+), which enable multiple antibacterial modes under near-infrared (NIR) light irradiation. The photothermal conversion efficiency and ROS generation of Pt0 core under NIR irradiation contribute to its significant photothermal and photodynamic antibacterial effects. Furthermore, the Pt2+ shells can release Pt2+ under the photothermal effect to achieve chemotherapeutic antibacterial activity. Ultimately, NIR-activated dvPtNPs lead to bacterial membrane rupture and RNA leakage, significantly reducing bacterial viability. Notably, dvPtNPs exhibit particularly strong bactericidal effects against E. coli and MRSA. These findings highlight the potential of dvPtNPs as a multifunctional platform for effective and targeted photothermal antibacterial therapies, combining both photothermal and chemotherapeutic effects.

Metal sulfide/oxide nanoparticles are employed in photothermal therapy (PTT) due to their cost-effectiveness compared to noble metals, semiconductor properties, compatibility with cellular systems, ease of preparation, and favorable photothermal conversion rates [243,270]. These nanoparticles exhibit promising potential for various biological applications, thanks to their low band gap, biochemical properties, thick atomic scale, significant spin–orbit coupling, and thermal stability [271,272]. Metal sulfide/oxide nanoparticles demonstrate a broad spectrum of antibacterial activity against bacteria, fungi, and viruses, making them suitable for sterilization purposes as well. Moreover, their chemical characteristics make them suitable for applications in both the biological and catalytic domains [273]. However, the therapeutic application of metal sulfide/oxide nanoparticles is limited due to concerns regarding their toxicity to specific mammalian cells and the potential for side effects in non-target cells [274]. A summary of the antibacterial properties of various types of metal sulfide/oxide nanomaterials is shown in Table 3 .

Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2), a two-dimensional transition metal dichalcogenide (TMD), is an attractive option for germ eradication due to its inherent antibacterial activity, which includes surface adhesion sterilization, photodynamic therapy (PDT) sterilization, and photothermal therapy (PTT) sterilization [282]. Recently, various two-dimensional nanomaterials such as MXenes, graphene and its derivatives, TMDs, and hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) have been utilized for antibacterial applications. These materials possess characteristics such as high surface area, excellent permeability to bacterial biofilms, ease of surface functionalization, high biocompatibility, and diverse antibacterial properties [283,284]. The antibacterial mechanisms of these nanomaterials involve physical contact, which compromises the integrity of the cell wall, oxidative stress resulting in the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), and light-induced antibacterial effects, such as photodynamic or photocatalytic and photothermal antibacterial activities [285,286]. MoS2 can adopt various morphologies, including nanoplates, nanotubes, nanowires, nanoflowers, nanorods, and quantum dots. Among them, chemically exfoliated MoS2 (ce-MoS2) sheets exhibit superior antibacterial capacity compared to raw MoS2 powder and aggregated two-dimensional (2D) ce-MoS2. This enhanced antibacterial performance is mainly attributed to the higher ROS generation and the larger contact area offered by the ce-MoS2 sheet [282].

In a study conducted by Feng et al., [287], it was observed that dual-light irradiation, as compared to individual irradiation at 660 nm visible light (VL) or 808 nm near-infrared (NIR), could induce synergistic photothermal and photodynamic effects of a chitosan-assisted MoS2 (CS@MoS2) hybrid coating within a short duration in vivo. This finding highlights the potential of dual-light irradiation for enhanced therapeutic outcomes. Yuan et al., synthesized a multipurpose coating composed of titanium dioxide, polydopamine, arginine-glycine-aspartic acid (PDA-RGD), and MoS2 (MoS2/PDA-RGD) [288]. This nanocomposite exhibited the ability to stimulate new bone growth in living organisms, even in contaminated environments. It also demonstrated strong antibacterial effects by disrupting bacterial cell membrane permeability and stability. Despite these advancements, there remains a scarcity of investigations focusing on functionalized or hybridized approaches to enhance MoS2′s antibacterial properties. Hu et al., developed a functionalized nanoplatform, 1-MT-Pt-PPDA@MoS2, which exhibited favorable attributes such as efficient photothermal conversion and NIR absorption, pH-induced and NIR laser-triggered controlled release, and the ability to inhibit cancer cell growth in combination with chemotherapy [289]. ( Fig. 12
A-E) These studies collectively shed light on the potential of MoS2-based materials and hybrid coatings for multifunctional applications, including antibacterial therapies, bone regeneration, and cancer treatment. Further research into functionalizing and hybridizing MoS2 nanomaterials holds promise for expanding their biomedical applications and improving their antibacterial efficacy.

Nanosized MoS2 exhibits a higher number of active sites compared to bulk MoS2, primarily due to the mechanical force applied during direct band gap engineering, which overcomes the van der Waals force [290]. MoS2 nanosheets perform exceptionally due to quantum confinement, resulting from their reduced dimensions [291]. Exfoliation is the most commonly used method to produce MoS2 nanosheets, which can be achieved through mechanical, chemical, liquid phase, or chemical vapor deposition (CVD) approaches [292]. Zhang et al., utilized the large surface area of MoS2 nanosheets to load them with penicillin [155,275]. In in vitro experiments, these nano complexes demonstrated effective antibacterial activity against various bacteria, including S. aureus, S. coli, and multidrug-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (MRPA). Yan et al., reported that chemically exfoliated MoS2 nanosheets (ce-MoS2) possess enhanced antibacterial properties due to their increased capability to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) and oxidize glutathione (GSH)[282]. The size and morphology of MoS2 materials significantly influence the contact area and charge transfer regulation between cells and the material. Ce-MoS2′s active edges and larger surface area facilitate greater interaction with bacteria, leading to more effective charge transfer regulation and excellent antibacterial effects. Therefore, MoS2 materials' size and morphological characteristics play a crucial role in cell-material interactions and charge transfer phenomena.

In a study by Wu et al., it was demonstrated that increasing concentrations of MoS2 nanosheets led to the disruption of bacterial cell membranes, increased production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), decreased cell viability, and alterations in relevant metabolite concentrations involved in various metabolic processes [293]. These processes encompass amino acid metabolism, protein biosynthesis, the urea cycle, and pyruvate metabolism, among others. Roy et al., developed a facile and environmentally friendly approach to synthesize chitosan-exfoliated MoS2 (CS-MoS2) nanosheets [294]. These nanosheets exhibited high biocompatibility with mammalian cells and displayed concentration- and duration-dependent anti-biofilm and antibacterial activities against both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria. The antibacterial mechanism of CS-MoS2 nanosheets involves biofilm damage, metabolic inactivation, and oxidative stress. In contrast to MoS2 synthesized using ultrasonication (US-MoS2) and hydrothermal (HY-MoS2) methods, Zhang et al., investigated the antibacterial properties of MoS2 produced through the intercalation technique (IN-MoS2) [295]. The IN-MoS2 exhibited superior antibacterial efficacy under white light, as well as excellent sterilizing effects, compact size, superior material properties, and higher metal content compared to the other synthesis methods [295]. Zhang et al., prepared MoS2@PDA-PEG/IgG nanosheets (MPPI NSs) for the treatment of focal infections caused by Staphylococcus aureus [276]. The MPPI NSs exhibited favorable photothermal characteristics, focused antibacterial capacity (with bacterial reduction exceeding 99.99%), accelerated healing properties, and no significant damage to mammalian cells was observed in vitro or in vivo. (Fig. 13
A-E) The findings highlight the safety of MoS2-based nanomaterials for antibacterial therapies, including minimal harm to mammalian cells.

MoS2 nanosheets have proven highly effective as antibacterial surface treatments. However, the antibacterial effects of MoS2 quantum dots (MoS2 QDs) have received limited attention in the literature. Unlike MoS2 nanosheets, which exhibit restricted charge carrier motion due to their semiconducting nature, MoS2 QDs display enhanced and more active charge carrier motion, owing to their smaller size [296]. MoS2 QDs possess several advantageous properties, including abundant active edges, ease of fabrication, quantum confinement, and unique surface characteristics, making them highly suitable for applications in the photocatalysis and biomedical sciences. Notably, compared to other 2D molybdenum nanomaterials, MoS2 QDs exhibit exceptional optical properties alongside significant biocompatibility [297]. Furthermore, in comparison to previous MoS2 nanoparticles, MoS2 QDs offer a larger surface-to-volume ratio, increased conducting and catalytically active edge areas, adjustable fluorescence emissions, and smaller dimensions [298]. Various synthesis techniques can be employed to produce MoS2 QDs, including top-down and bottom-up approaches such as thermal ablation, liquid exfoliation, mechanical exfoliation, chemical exfoliation, electrochemical exfoliation, solvothermal methods, electrochemical methods, and emulsion methods [272]. Hydrothermal routes involving small molecules containing thiols and molybdate salts as precursors are commonly employed for MoS2 QD synthesis [299].

MoS2-based nanomaterials can maximize photocatalytic performance by reducing the lateral size and thickness of their layers, which induces a transition from an indirect to a direct band gap, as highlighted by previous research [300]. Tian et al., conducted a study that elucidated the exceptional antibacterial effect of MoS2 quantum dots (QDs). It was found that under solar light, MoS2 nanosheets facilitated the self-separation of electron-hole pairs, leading to the disruption of bacterial antioxidative defense mechanisms [301]. The coordinated molecular interactions between electrons and holes resulted in the formation of electron-hole pairs, which subsequently separated into individual electrons and holes. In vivo experiments demonstrated that MoS2 QDs significantly enhanced wound healing, and it was evident that ROS-mediated oxidative stress played a pivotal role in the antibacterial mechanism [301]. These findings emphasize the potential of MoS2 QDs, for their remarkable photocatalytic and antibacterial properties, thereby contributing to the development of advanced materials for biomedical applications.

MoS2 nanoflowers, characterized by their petal-like structures at the nanoscale in the range of 100–500 nm, offer several advantages in terms of surface reaction efficiency and surface-to-body ratio [302]. The three-dimensional (3D) nanoflower structure further enhances these properties, resulting in a compact yet highly surface-active material with improved carrier immobility and charge transfer [303]. RRecent research has highlighted the broad potential applications of 3D MoS2 nanosystems due to their high aspect ratios and abundant active edges [304]. Xu al et. successfully synthesized MoS2 nanoflowers and MoS2 nanosheets through potentiostat-controlled electrolysis using Mo electrodes at various currents. MoS2 nanoflowers, with their larger specific surface area (area-to-volume ratio) compared to MoS2 sheets, exhibit stronger antibacterial capabilities [305]. This increased ratio may induce higher oxidative stress in bacterial cells. Furthermore, MoS2 nanoflowers demonstrate superior capacity for oxidizing glutathione (GSH) and offer a larger interacting surface [305]. In another study conducted by Mutalik et al., the synthesis of metallized 1 T-MoS2 nanofibrils and semiconducting 2H-MoS2 nanofibrils was successfully achieved using a solvothermal process and annealing system [306]. The investigation focused on evaluating the reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation of metallized 1 T-MoS2 nanofibrils upon light irradiation. It was found that metallic 1 T-MoS2 nanofibrils exhibited higher ROS production compared to semiconducting 2H-MoS2 nanofibrils under light irradiation. Consequently, metallic 1 T-MoS2 nanofibrils displayed greater antibacterial activity under light illumination compared to semiconducting 2H-MoS2 nanofibrils [306]. Certain nanomaterials possess peroxidase-like activity and find applications in antibacterial systems. MoS2, known for its inherent peroxidase-like catalytic properties, demonstrates excellent biocompatibility and exhibits significant photothermal conversion capacity in the near-infrared (NIR) region, making it potentially advantageous for photothermal therapy (PTT) applications [306,307].

Metal compounds such as copper sulfide (CuS) and molybdenum sulfide (MoS2) have shown promising antimicrobial properties as photothermal nanomaterials [308]. CuS is commonly synthesized using a facile hydrothermal method. Hu et al., demonstrated the excellent antibacterial characteristics (killing rate of S. aureus was 95% at 100 μg/mL concentration) and acceptable photothermal conversion rates (estimated to be 42.1%) of polyvinyl pyrrolidone-coated CuS nanocrystals (CuS-PVP) nanoparticles synthesized via a simple hydrothermal technique using CuCl2 [309]. Another study conducted by Huang's team involved the hydrothermal synthesis of bovine serum albumin (BSA)-encapsulated CuS nanoparticles (CuS-BSA) [310]. These nanoparticles exhibited strong absorbance in the near-infrared region, excellent photostability, biocompatibility, and achieved a sterilization rate exceeding 80% upon near-infrared irradiation, effectively destroying bacterial activity. Additionally, Li et al., observed that the controlled release of Cu ions from hydrogels under NIR promoted angiogenesis, fibroblast growth, and granulation tissue production, facilitating wound healing [311]. In a study conducted by Zhao et al., diabetic infected focal infection tissues were treated with bovine serum albumin stabilized CuS (BSA-CuS) based photothermal therapy in vivo, which resulted in faster wound healing [312]. Dai et al., synthesized positively charged copper sulfide/poly(5-(2-ethyl acrylate)-4-methyl-thiazole-g-butyl)/nanoclusters (PATA3-C4@CuS) [277]. These nanoclusters demonstrated the ability to electrostatically attract bacteria and destroy them through dual-modal photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT), with thiazole derivatives acting as bacterial tracers. Dai et al., found that these nanoclusters significantly suppressed various levofloxacin-resistant bacteria and accelerated wound healing in vivo without harming dermal tissue [277]. Qiao et al., utilized a copper sulfide nanodot (CuS NDs) nanosystem for the treatment of chronic non-healing infected wounds, employing CuS photothermal and photodynamic effects [278]. (Fig. 14
A-B) The nanosystem exhibited effective killing of drug-resistant pathogens, showed low cytotoxicity, and released Cu2+ ions to promote rapid wound healing. Yang et al., synthesized plasmonic Cu3SnS4 nanosheets (Cu3SnS4 NFs) with notable advantages including facile preparation, cost-effectiveness, high photothermal conversion efficiency (55.7%), and favorable biocompatibility and biosafety [279]. Subsequently, Cu3SnS4 NFs were employed in the controlled photothermal antimicrobial therapy of E. coli and methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus-infected wounds using near-infrared (NIR) radiation at 808 nm. (Fig. 14 C) The findings demonstrated the effective eradication of bacterial infections and accelerated wound healing through Cu3SnS4 NFs' synergistic antibacterial mechanisms. These mechanisms encompassed the physical eradication of negatively charged bacteria by positively charged Cu3SnS4 NFs through electrostatic interactions, generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) capable of mediating oxidative damage to bacteria(Fig. 14 D), and the induction of photothermal effects to dismantle bacterial biofilms [279]. (Fig. 14 E) Furthermore, Cu3SnS4 upregulated the expression of VEGF, CD31, and HIF-1α, which facilitated the formation of granulation tissue, endothelial cell angiogenesis, and collagen deposition, thus promoting expedited wound healing. (Fig. 14 C) This study exemplifies the significance of photothermal synergistic antimicrobial action, wherein the interaction between different mechanisms not only reinforces their efficacy but also engenders the acquisition of novel beneficial functions.

Magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (NPs), such as Fe2O3 and Fe3O4, represent a promising class of metal sulfide/oxide nanomaterials for various applications [180]. In the field of photothermal therapy and antibacterial treatments, magnetic iron oxide NPs are often combined with other photothermal agents (PTAs) to form composite nanomaterials, enabling effective therapeutic outcomes [313]. Through a hydrothermal technique and in-situ growth, Wei et al., successfully synthesized a reusable Fe3O4@MoS2-1 %Ag nanozyme, composed of MoS2, iron oxide, and silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) [280]. They demonstrated that 808 nm near-infrared (NIR) irradiation induced local hyperthermia and increased peroxidase activity, resulting in a broad range of antibacterial properties against various bacterial species, including drug-resistant bacteria and fungi. Fe3O4@MoS2-1 %Ag exhibited the ability to damage Escherichia coli (E. coli) by releasing toxic Ag+ ions and hydroxyl radicals (•OH) to attack bacterial biofilms under NIR irradiation. In another study, Fe3O4/CNT/Gent nanocomposites were developed for the treatment of methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA)-induced osteomyelitis. These nanocomposites consisted of mesoporous Fe3O4 nanospheres combined with functionalized carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and gentamicin. Fe3O4/CNT/Gent nanocomposites allowed for the NIR-mediated controlled release of antibiotics and the NIR-mediated bacterial clearance through a microwave-caloric-chemotherapy (MCCT) system. The nanocomposites exhibited the capability to precisely capture bacteria, enabling magnetic targeting of the nanocapturer [314]. To create NIR-irradiated photothermal antibacterial nanomaterials, Jeong et al., combined Fe3O4 nanoparticles with catechol/sultone conjugated polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) and enclosed them in a poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (PEDOT) shell [281]. The Fe3O4@CPVPs:PEDOT (1:100) nanocomposites demonstrated high photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE), with values of 24.58% and 51.14% for Fe3O4 NPs and Fe3O4@CPVPs:PEDOT (1:100), respectively [1,281]. The Fe3O4@CPVPs:PEDOT (1:100) nanocomposites exhibited superior antibacterial efficacy compared to Fe3O4 alone, achieving nearly 100% eradication of both Gram-positive (E. coli) and Gram-negative (S. aureus) bacteria under 808 nm NIR irradiation for 5 min [281]. These findings highlight the high photothermal heating efficiency of Fe3O4@CPVPs:PEDOT (1:100) for rapid bacterial eradication in both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacterial species.

Polymeric nanomaterials such as polyaniline (PANI), polypyridine (Ppy), poly(3,4-ethylene dioxythiophene) (PEDOT), and polydopamine (PDA) have been extensively studied [238,247,315,316]. These polymeric nanomaterials exhibit favorable properties including biocompatibility, high photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE), excellent photostability, high drug loading capacity, facile synthesis, and negligible toxicity in both in vitro and in vivo settings, thereby positioning them as promising candidates for photothermal therapy (PTT) [317–319]. Furthermore, the conjugation of targeted ligands to these nanoparticles enhances their targeted delivery to specific cells and improves their specific binding ability. A summary of the antibacterial properties of various types of polymeric nanomaterials is shown in Table 4 .

Polymeric nanomaterials incorporating polyaniline (PANI) have garnered significant attention due to their conductivity, non-cytotoxic nature, and cost-effectiveness, making them attractive for investigating cellular proliferation [112,327]. The synthesis of polyaniline nanoparticles involves the protonation reaction of aniline salts, utilizing aniline as a monomer, hydrochloric acid (HCl) as an oxidizer, and ammonium persulfate as the starting material [317,328]. Yang et al., employed chemical oxidation polymerization to produce doped polyaniline nanomaterials, which exhibited improved solubility in organic solutions and a more uniform size distribution [329]. Through the protonation of emeraldine base (EB) into emeraldine salt (ES) in an acidic environment, polyaniline demonstrated substantial absorption in the near-infrared (NIR) range, with a significant red shift in the absorption peak. Yang et al.'s subsequent research elucidated that the photothermal heating induced by PANPs under NIR laser irradiation inhibits DNA and/or RNA production, degrades proteins, and disrupts cytoskeletal filaments, ultimately leading to cell death [329]. However, another study indicated that the conversion of EB to ES occurs at a very low pH (< 3), which is typically lower than the pH of the microenvironment surrounding focal infection tissues, leading to a diminished photothermal therapy (PTT) effect near the infection site [330]. Zhou et al., employed a hydrothermal process to fabricate homogeneous polyaniline nanomaterials with F127 modification (F-PANPs), which exhibited desirable characteristics such as appropriate size, strong NIR absorption, high photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE) of 48.5%, good water solubility, high photothermal stability, effective therapeutic action in clinical settings, minimal cytotoxicity, and efficient coupling agent for in vivo PTT applications [315]. Previous studies have proposed various techniques to enhance PANI protonation in photothermal therapy (PTT) by self-doping and increasing the charge transfer rate, each with its advantages and disadvantages [331]. For instance, the introduction of acid groups to PANI backbones resulted in a self-doping effect, leading to strong absorption across a wide pH range [331]. However, self-doped PANI could only be dispersed in highly alkaline solutions, limiting its suitability for biological therapy [332]. In another approach, Au@PANI demonstrated high NIR irradiation absorbance within a relatively neutral pH range (6.5–8.0) due to an improved electric charge transfer rate. Nonetheless, recent studies have raised concerns about the long-term biodegradability and potential hazards associated with the introduction of nonbiodegradable Au into compounds [333,334]. Furthermore, it was observed that PANI alone exhibited a significantly lower pH response (pH<4) compared to BSA-PANI assemblies (pH<7) due to the self-doping effect facilitated by interparticle acid-base interactions between PANI's imine moieties and BSA's carboxyl groups. The presence of BSA reduced PANI's deprotonation level and facilitated its transition from the emeraldine base (EB) to the emeraldine salt (ES) form at low acidic pH values (pH<7) [335,336]. Considering the excellent biocompatibility and adaptability of the BSA-PANI composite, it holds high potential for PTT application in biomedical fields. In a recent study by Tang et al., polyaniline (PANI)-poly (pstyrenesulfonic acid) (PSS) (PANI:PSS) was non-invasively translocated into bacterial cell membranes, resulting in an intracellular photothermal effect that physically destroyed the cell's active components and led to cell death [320]. (Fig. 15
A-E).

The conjugated polymer nanomaterials, specifically poly(3,4-ethylene dioxythiophene) (PEDOT), exhibit distinctive characteristics that make them highly suitable for photothermal applications. With a size of 17.2 nm, PEDOT nanoparticles possess broad near-infrared (NIR) absorbance bands spanning 700–1250 nm and achieve a remarkable photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE) of 71.1%[321]. For the optimization of PEDOT: ICG@PEG-GTA in combined photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT), a two-step synthesis approach was employed. Initially, PEDOT nanoparticles were synthesized using an environmentally friendly hydrothermal process, followed by the sequential addition of polyethylene poly (sodium-p-styrene sulfonate) (PSS) and indocyanine green (ICG) solution, polyethylene glycol (PEG)–NH2 solution, and glutaraldehyde (GTA). The final synthesis of PEDOT: ICG@PEG-GTA was accomplished through conjugative bonding [321]. PEDOT's intrinsic photothermal effect is observed in the near-infrared II (NIR II) region, facilitating PTT applications. Moreover, the surface modification of PEDOT with ICG via electrostatic attraction enables the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and facilitates PDT under irradiation in the NIR I region. The combined PEDOT PTT and ICG PDT exhibited impressive antibacterial efficacy against E. coli and S. aureus when subjected to 1064 nm and 808 nm wavelength radiation, respectively. Notably, PEDOT: ICG@PEG-GTA demonstrated excellent sterilization rates (approximately 99%) in both the NIR I and II regions, while exhibiting high PCE, outstanding photostability, and minimal cytotoxicity [321]. In a study conducted by Ko et al., a novel photothermal nanocomposite hydrogel composed of agarose and PEDOT decorated with poly(styrene-sulfonate) (PEDOT: PSS) was developed [238]. The authors found that a mere 2 min of NIR exposure to PEDOT: PSS/agarose effectively eradicated S. aureus and E. coli bacteria. The rapid and significant temperature increase of 24.5 °C within 100 s of NIR exposure (808 nm, 2.0 W/cm^2) contributed to the efficient elimination of pathogenic bacteria within a short time period. PEDOT: PSS/agarose exhibited light-induced thermal sensitivity, light-induced self-healing capabilities, and superior antibacterial properties. In another study by Li et al., the nanogels (PEDOT@PNIPAAm-Cur) was created by precipitating and polymerizing poly (3,4-ethylene dioxythiophene) nanoparticles with curcumin [322]. The nanoparticles were then enclosed in Poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAAm), which is a thermosensitive gel, and eventually synthesized the PEDOT@PNIPAAm-Cur. The PEDOT@PNIPAAm-Cur has exceptional characteristics in scavenging oxidative stress and photothermal effect, which are used to inactivate bacterial pathogens. Upon increasing the temperature, S. aureus and E. coli were efficiently eliminated, and mortality was 90%. The temperature-sensitive effects of the PNIPAAm increased, and the association between the networks of curcumin and the PNIPAAm further weakened, resulting in the release of curcumin [322]. MacNeill et al., showed that altering the aspect ratio of PEDOT nanoparticles boosts their ability to absorb more NIR irradiation and raises their heat transfer performance by an average of 10 ℃ [337]. Therefore, compared to PEDOT: PSS nanoparticles (100–200 nm), PEDOT nanotubes (200–400 nm in diameter, 4–10 m in length) can absorb more NIR irradiation and get higher temperatures.

Polydopamine (PDA), a mussel-inspired material derived from mussel adhesive proteins, possesses remarkable adhesion properties and offers several advantages, including a simple preparation process, high biocompatibility, and the ability to undergo functionalization through photothermal conversion. The presence of catechol and primary amine groups in PDA contributes to its exceptional adhesion, metal coordination, and antioxidant properties [324,338]. Drawing inspiration from mussels, PDA coatings consisting of polymerized dopamine in a mildly alkaline environment exhibit flexible biomimetic surface modification and find extensive applications in the immobilization of macromolecular complexes [339]. The catechol component of PDA, as a catechol-derived system, provides effective sites for modification through metal chelation, various chemical reactions, and the co-construction of compounds with other biomaterials [340,341]. Furthermore, PDA can be modified with different ligands to serve as a drug delivery carrier for active targeting purposes [342]. Li et al., synthesized a cationic polyethyleneimine/anionic pectin (CPAP) comprising PDA@Cu nanoparticles for photothermal antibacterial applications as a photothermal agent [323]. (Fig. 16
A-B) The hybrid hydrogel captured and eradicated bacteria and prevented nanoparticle polymerization under near-infrared (NIR) laser irradiation through electrostatic adsorption and photothermal conversion. In a study conducted by Qi et al., a multi-step approach was employed to develop a novel photothermal antibacterial platform, CG/PDA@Ag [244]. This platform involved the growth of silver nanocrystals on polydopamine (PDA) nanoparticles, followed by encapsulation of the PDA@Ag nanocomposites within a cationic guar gum (CG) hydrogel matrix using a one-pot blending method. In vitro experiments were performed to assess the antibacterial efficacy of the CG/PDA@Ag platform against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus. (Fig. 16 C) The results demonstrated excellent bactericidal rates and reduced bacterial viability. Specifically, the platform exhibited a bactericidal rate of 99.9% against Escherichia coli and 99.8% against Staphylococcus aureus, highlighting its potent antimicrobial properties. (Fig. 16 D) Moreover, the platform exhibited superior antimicrobial properties in vivo, indicating its potential for practical applications. The incorporation of a hydrogel matrix further enhanced its bacterial trapping ability, underscoring its overall effectiveness in combating bacterial infections [244].

Polydopamine (PDA) nanoparticles have demonstrated several desirable characteristics, including low cytotoxicity, minimal residual bioactivity, high photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE), excellent biocompatibility, water solubility, and the ability to undergo chemical modifications and precise targeting of cells [343,344]. These attributes make PDA nanomaterials promising candidates for targeted pathogen eradication and photothermal therapy [345,346]. PDA's broad absorption spectrum contributes to its superior photothermal conversion efficiency, as well as its electrical and chemical properties. PDA can form various conjugated and donor–acceptor configurations through its indole nanostructures and non-covalent interactions, such as p-p, cation-p, and hydrogen bonding [347,348]. When coated on specific nanosystems, PDA imparts unique features to the nano-biomaterials, including minimal cellular toxicity, targeted adhesion, excellent biocompatibility, strong absorption of near-infrared irradiation, high photothermal stability, and high PCE [336,341,342,344,349,350]. Consequently, such nanosystems can find broader applications in biological therapy, providing increased innovation and potential. Yuan et al., presented a study in which Ti implants surfaces were modified by incorporating polydopamine nanoparticles (MPDA) and photosensitizers (ICG) [324]. The modified surfaces exhibited a remarkable 95.4% efficiency in removing bacterial biofilms under NIR laser irradiation. Following the removal of bacterial biofilms, the Ti implant complexes (Ti-M/I/RGD) not only demonstrated bactericidal activity but also facilitated efficient osteogenesis and osseointegration, eliminating the need for invasive surgery or therapies. The sensitivity of S. aureus in bacterial biofilms to medium hyperthermia (50 °C) was observed due to the destruction of bacterial membranes by ROS generated by ICG.

Vancomycin chemically modified polydopamine nanoparticles (PDA-PEG-Van) were employed to demonstrate their photothermal performance with exceptional and precise targeting capabilities against MRSA under limited NIR-laser irradiation. These nanoparticles established multivalent hydrogen-bond connections via MRSA cell walls, enabling effective killing of MRSA through localized hyperthermia while causing minimal damage to normal mammalian cells [325]. Xu et al., demonstrated the superiority of PDA nanomaterial over peroxidase enzymatic technology or individual photothermal therapy in killing both S. aureus (95.2%) and E. coli (96%) at mild photothermal temperatures of 45 ℃ within a short period [326]. Another study by Xu et al., reported an antibacterial effect using a hybrid coating of polydopamine (PDA) on hydroxyapatite (HAp) incorporated with gold nanoparticles (Au-Hap). The PDA@Au-HAp NPs system, in conjunction with low H2O2 concentrations, promoted collagen production, granulation, and accelerated wound healing under NIR laser irradiation [326]. Moreover, when PDA is combined with other antimicrobial materials such as NO gas, Ag NPs, antibiotics, Cu2+, CBNs, QAS, and graphite oxide through chemical reactions or physical interactions, there is a significant improvement in the antibacterial properties compared to antibiotics and phototherapy using individual photothermal agents alone [247,351]. There has been significant improvement in the antibacterial properties of PDA composite material compared to antibiotics and phototherapy with individual photothermal agents alone [326]. (Fig. 17
A-D) Antimicrobial agents can be released in complex systems, utilizing the high-temperature output of the PDA nanosystem to effectively damage bacterial biofilms. The study successfully demonstrated the efficient antibacterial ability and reliable biocompatibility of the PDA composite material [38,326].

In 2011, researchers reported the emergence of MXenes, a novel class of nanomaterials with a two-dimensional (2D) structure and characteristics similar to graphene [352]. Since then, MXenes have gained significant attention from researchers, who explore their potential applications and foresee them as the next disruptive material. The 2D “MXenes” family comprises transition metal carbides, nitrides, and carbonitrides [353]. These materials are represented by the chemical formula Mn+1XnTx (where n ranges from 1 to 5), with M denoting early transition metals such as Ti, Mn, Mo, Sc, V, Nb, Cr, and others, X originally representing C or N, and Tx referring to surface terminations through functional groups (primarily OH, O, and/or F) that substitute for the strong Al-M bonds in the Mn+1Axn phase. MXenes consist of planar nanolayers, typically a few micrometers in size, with only a few atomic layers between them [354]. The synthesis of MXenes involves the selective etching of Al layers (referred to as A layers in MAX) using anhydrous hydrofluoric acid (HF) at controlled indoor temperatures. This etching process leads to the transformation of the layered 3D solid into a 2D solid by removing the A-element from the MAX phase through surface terminal substitution with O, OH, and F groups [354].

Recently, various approaches have been employed for the synthesis of MXenes and their derivatives, including electrochemical preparation [355], chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [356], chemical etching [357], hydrothermal synthesis [358], the urea glass process [359], and bioinspired/biomimetic methods [360]. These techniques enable the successful fabrication of monolayers, few layers, and multilayer MXene nanostructures through both top-down and bottom-up approaches. Among these techniques, chemical vapor deposition and chemical etching are commonly utilized for MXene synthesis [361]. Wet etching of MXenes results in a hydrophilic nature and alterations in the terminations of the transition metal layer in the electronic energy band [362]. The process involves immersing the precursor material in a chemical etching solution to remove the A-layer constituents, followed by sonication to separate the material into individual nanolayers, although sonication may introduce structural flaws and reduce MXene size. In contrast, top-down chemical etching involves the division of bulk materials, such as the MAX phase, into multiple layers with high crystallinity [356]. The bottom-up method allows for the fabrication of 2D nanomaterials with minimal structural flaws and larger lateral sizes by controlling the deposition of multilayered epitaxial films [363]. However, the large lateral dimensions of MXene nanomaterials synthesized using conventional techniques limit their biomedical applications. To address this, Lin et al., developed a two-step exfoliation process involving HF etching and TPAOH intercalation to reduce MXene size [364]. The resulting Ti3C2 nanosheets, with dimensions of approximately 100 nm and atomic-thin thickness, exhibit strong near-infrared (NIR) absorbance and high photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE) upon NIR-light radiation (808 nm), attributed to localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effects [365].

MXenes are synthesized using a top-down approach, where the precursor phase known as the MAX phase (Mn+1AXn) is utilized [366]. The bonding within the MAX phase involves a combination of metallic, chemical, and ionic bonds between the M and X elements, while a metallic bond is present between M and A. Besides the MAX phase, other layered compositions have also been identified as precursor phases for MXene synthesis [367]. Due to the diverse nature of chemistry bonding, it is possible to selectively etch specific elements from layered precursors, including the MAX phase or other compositions, to produce 2D MXenes. However, the MAX phase requires more etching compared to materials like graphene or molybdenum disulfide, which are bonded by van der Waals forces [368]. In the conventional top-down chemical etching approach, MXenes are synthesized in two stages. The first stage involves the selective removal of the “A” layered elements from the MAX or non-MAX phases, where A represents a group of elements primarily from groups 13 or 14 of the periodic table (e.g., Al, Si) to obtain multilayered MXenes [369,370]. However, chemical etching techniques involving hydrogen fluoride can damage skin and other bodily cells. To mitigate this, researchers have explored the use of hydrochloric acid (HCl) with fluoride salts, which allows for the in situ synthesis of HF, thus reducing harm to the body [371]. The situ-synthesis of HF successfully prevents the body from suffering more harm.

MXene-based nanomaterials possess exceptional characteristics that make them highly attractive for various applications. These qualities include a significant specific surface area, high aspect ratio, extremely low cytotoxicity (Fig. 18
A-F), excellent biocompatibility, abundant surface functional groups, atomic-thin thickness, remarkable water solubility, outstanding photothermal conversion performance, and exceptional optical, physical, and chemical properties. MXene nanomaterials also exhibit impressive capability in converting and confining electromagnetic waves [372–375]. These unique attributes contribute to the remarkable photothermal antibacterial activity of MXenes, which is further enhanced by the shunt localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effect [376]. Furthermore, several basic elements utilized in MXene-based materials, including typical MAX phases, have demonstrated superior biocompatibility [364]. Despite extensive research on MXene-based materials, certain characteristics have not fully met the diverse demands of various applications [377]. To enable their future use in biomedical applications, the development of multifunctional nanosystems for complex surface modification is imperative [374]. These modifications aim to enhance the properties of MXene-based materials, particularly those utilized in pharmaceutical packaging, drug delivery devices, biosensors, pharmacotherapy nanoplatforms, biomedical imaging, photothermal therapy (PTT), and interactions with hydrophilic biomolecules [378,379]. Consequently, the surfaces of MXene-based materials need to be modified with specific functional groups, such as hydroxyl, fluoride, and oxygen ions, to achieve enhancements [377].

MXene-based materials' antimicrobial effectiveness is influenced by factors such as photothermal conversion efficiency, rate of warming, and maximum temperature reached [365,380]. Notably, Ti3C2Tx exhibits a broad absorption range from 200 to 1500 nm. This characteristic allows for efficient absorption of radiation energy across various wavelengths, which is rapidly converted into heat through the photothermal conversion of MXenes [376]. A separate study demonstrated that Ti3C2Tx achieved an impressive photothermal conversion efficiency of 100% [381]. Furthermore, Ti3C2Tx outperformed other materials under investigation in terms of photothermal conversion efficiency, surpassing Gold nanorods (21%), Prussian blue (41.4%), Cu9S5 (25.7%), and graphitic nanocapsules (37%) [382]. Moreover, MXene-based materials attained higher maximum temperatures compared to other materials studied, including zirconium carbide (ZrC), graphitic carbon (GC), and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [126,376].

A consistent and repeatable photothermal response was observed when Ti3C2Tx was exposed to high-power density NIR-light at 808 nm, even at a low concentration of Ti3C2Tx (10 μg mL-1). The temperature increased by 90 °C within 500–600 s, demonstrating reusability, high efficiency, and photothermal stability [382]. Photothermal antibacterial activity of MXenes was investigated to understand their effects on bacterial proliferation. MXene-based materials suppress bacterial proliferation by destroying crucial organelles and intracellular components under light irradiation [383]. This includes damage to cellular membranes, cell walls, nucleus mimics, DNA and RNA, ribosomes, and biological enzymes [384]. Hyperthermia caused by heat severely damages lipids, proteins, and polysaccharides in cellular membranes, leading to increased membrane permeability. Consequently, cell membrane damage disrupts barrier function, resulting in unregulated intracellular substances movement and homeostatic imbalance [385]. The susceptibility of cell wall peptidoglycans to disintegration varies between Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria due to differences in peptidoglycan thickness, resulting in varying responses to MXene's antibacterial activity [386,387]. Additionally, TiO2-induced oxidative stress on the bacterial cell wall enhances Ti3C2Tx's antibacterial activity by promoting oxidative radicals [388]. Proteins in bacterial cells, which serve as cellular structures or biological enzymes, undergo heat-induced denaturation and aggregation due to hyperthermia [389]. Numerous mechanisms can cause protein functionality loss. Furthermore, 2D-structured MXenes can be synthesized as quantum dots, offering advantages such as high hydrophilicity, optical and chemical stability, ease of functionalization, high extinction coefficients, and ultra-high PCE [390–392]. Rosenkranz et al., investigated the difference between few-layer (FX) and multi-layer (MX) Ti3C2Tx nanosheets for photothermal therapy (PTT) antibacterial activity against E. coli and S. aureus under 808 nm laser treatment. 00 g/mL of MX eradicated bacteria completely [393]. However, at a higher concentration of 100 g/mL, FX showed similar results to MX with superior photoconversion efficiency and significantly lower cytotoxicity to eukaryotic cells [393]. Under near-infrared light radiation at 808 nm, Ti3C2 MXenes suspension exhibited significant bactericidal effects against 15 species of bacteria within 20 min, including resistant strains such as vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus and methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus [394]. The physical destruction of biofilm structures primarily occurs through contact and photothermal effects.

Complexes of MXenes with other nanomaterials enhance the antimicrobial effect. Li et al., demonstrated the homogeneous loading of 100 nm to 500 nm CuS nanoparticles onto multilayer Ti3C2Tx surfaces, resulting in the formation of MXenes/CuS composites (Ti3C2Tx@CuS) through a straightforward hydrothermal process [395]. The improved photocatalytic activity of CuS generates more separate electrons and Cu2+ ions, which react with water and oxygen in the bacterial solution to produce more reactive oxygen species (ROS). Additionally, CuS's enhanced photothermal efficiency leads to increased heat generation. Under near-infrared radiation at 808 nm, 0.5 mg/mL Ti3C2Tx@CuS exhibits antibacterial efficacy of above 99% against both E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus. Li et al., also synthesized MXenes@polyvinyl alcohol (MXenes@PVA) hydrogels, which demonstrated photothermal action in inhibiting Staphylococcus aureus growth in infection wounds under near-infrared light at a wavelength of 808 nm [396]. These hydrogels also stimulated wound healing. Several novel and efficient MXenes and their composites have been developed and applied in the antibacterial field. However, the physiological effects and potential toxicity of MXenes and their composites still remain unclear. Further exploration and study of these compounds are necessary. Liu et al., successfully developed an optimized photothermal agent known as MXenes-M5C4 (Mo4VC4), which exhibits desirable properties for photothermal applications. This agent demonstrates a high photothermal conversion efficiency, reaching an impressive value of 45.5% [397]. Furthermore, MXenes-M5C4 demonstrates low cytotoxicity, minimal inflammatory reactions, and excellent biocompatibility, ensuring its suitability for biomedical applications. Through the combined action of photothermal therapy and chemodynamic therapy (PTT-CDT), synergistic effects are achieved in the presence of H2O2 and NIR-II irradiation. This approach leads to sustained high temperatures and the generation of a substantial quantity of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) [397]. As a result, Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) and Pseudomonas aeruginosa are effectively inactivated, while abscess regression and resorption are accelerated. Furthermore, wound healing processes are expedited, contributing to enhanced therapeutic outcomes.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are a class of novel porous antibacterial and multifunctional materials. These materials are composed of multi-dentate organic ligands linked to metal ions or metal clusters, forming infinite network linkage structures through self-assembled ligand bonds or covalent bonds [398]. MOFs are considered third-generation topologically structured antibacterial drugs by the majority of researchers [399,400]. Their key antibacterial characteristics include highly efficient bactericidal activity, controlled release of antibiotics, encapsulation of antibacterial coatings on polymer materials, and synergistic antibacterial effects in combination with other antibacterial nanoparticles [401]. MOFs find applications in various fields such as tumor therapy, nanocarriers for drug delivery, disease detection, bioactive catalysts, and direct utilization as antibacterial agents [400,402,403].

The metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) possess a range of characteristic features that make them highly attractive for various applications. These include high porosity, biocompatibility, high photothermal conversion efficiency, large specific surface area, facile incorporation of metal ions, ease of surface functionalization through functional attachment, versatile substance transport, flexible topological channels, adjustable ion release rate, and diverse pore shapes and architectures [404,405]. By carefully designing and adjusting the ligand structures and morphologies, specific physicochemical characteristics of MOFs can be tailored to optimize their porosity and specific surface area, allowing for regulation of the composition and size of the pores [401,406]. For instance, Lü et al., employed a UV–Vis-NIR light source to activate radical anions (RAs) in a perylenediimide metal–organic framework, thereby enhancing its near-infrared photothermal conversion efficiency [407]. (Fig. 19
A-B) Among the antimicrobial compounds, metal ions or metal oxide nanoparticles have garnered significant attention due to their antimicrobial activity via various mechanisms [408,409]. When these nanoparticles are deposited on bacterial cell membranes, they can physically disrupt the membrane structure, thereby interfering with the bacteria's physiological metabolism and leading to their demise. Some metal ions or metal oxide nanoparticles, such as CuO, ZnMgO (Fig. 19 C), TiO2, and ZnO, induce oxidative stress and cause bacterial death through oxidative protein denaturation [410–412]. Additionally, certain nanoparticles can convert light energy into heat energy, exerting a photothermal effect that eliminates bacteria, as seen with gold nanoparticles [413]. Metal ions or metal oxide nanoparticles exhibit remarkable antibacterial effects against a wide range of bacteria, including drug-resistant strains and common bacterial species [409]. MOFs can release metal ions, organic linkers, or trapped antimicrobial compounds under controlled conditions, thereby exerting antibacterial effects [414,415]. However, the potential toxicity of metal ions like Ag+ and Co2+ to human tissues restricts their use as carriers for injectable or inhaled antimicrobial drugs, such as cephalosporin antibiotics and gentamicin. Metal ion-controlled-release antimicrobials demonstrate potent antibacterial capabilities for localized applications [416,417]. Selecting MOFs synthesized from more biocompatible metal ions, such as zinc, reduces both metal ion toxicity and the toxicity of the final product [418,419]. MOFs also leverage the synergistic effect of metal ions and organic ligands for antibacterial purposes [420]. Organic ligands are crucial components of MOFs and serve as carriers for ligand molecules. The internal structural space of MOFs offers a significant surface volume and a distinct composition structure suitable for loading ligand molecules [401]. Therefore, MOFs have the potential to function as carriers for additional antibacterial drugs, capitalizing on their unique structural advantages [421]. In addition to its application in the antimicrobial domain, metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) exhibit considerable potential as photocatalytic materials, enabling the realization of synergistic antimicrobial effects through the combination of photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) [422]. By harnessing their ability to generate heat and free radicals, MOFs offer a promising avenue for integrating PTT and PDT modalities, thereby amplifying the antimicrobial efficacy and expanding the scope of their multifunctional applications. MOFs have been identified as excellent carriers for potent antibacterial composites [423]. T They exhibit various antimicrobial mechanisms, including gas therapy-based bactericidal effects, bioactive enzyme activity, direct interaction of metal active sites with bacteria, and synergistic antimicrobial effects when multiple active agents are combined [424,425]. These active agents can be introduced to MOFs through organic ligands, and the presence of porphyrin derivatives prevents the aggregation of these ligands, resulting in enhanced triplet state lifespan and quantum yield of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [426]. Moreover, certain MOFs can function as photosensitizers, generating antibacterial compounds such as H2 and ROS when exposed to light [423].

In addition to their efficacy, the safety of agents administered in vivo is of utmost importance. Cytotoxicity within a controlled range is critical. Various factors influence substances' toxicity, including composition, dosage dependence, structural stability, particle size, morphological structure, and surface functionalization [403,427]. Smaller-size MOFs exhibit higher toxicity compared to larger-size MOFs, despite the former being more likely to cross cell membranes and undergo decomposition [428]. Surface functionalization of MOFs enables interaction with diverse nanoparticles, reducing their toxic properties [427]. The selection of metal ion/cluster frameworks and organic ligand morphologies can be carefully made to minimize MOF toxicity for biomedical applications [428]. MOFs' structural stability serves as an indicator of their in vivo safety, as it determines the release of metal ions/clusters, linkers, and organic ligands that may damage nearby healthy tissues [429]. The structural stability can be altered by changing their morphology, structure, size, and composition [430]. Those changes result in the controlled release of metal ions and clusters, linkers, and organic ligands while also controlling the stability through controlled structural disintegration [431]. By reducing medication toxicity in surrounding normal tissues and enabling controlled release specifically at the site of infection, undesirable side effects of endogenous accumulation can be effectively prevented [429]. Several techniques have been documented for MOF production, with hydrothermal/solvothermal methods being the most commonly used, involving synthesis at high pressure and temperature in a complex solvent. Other available methods include dry gel transformation, electrochemical creation, mechanochemical formulation, microwave-assisted fabrication, ion heating synthesis, electrochemical fabrication, and microfluidic conversion [432,433].

MOF nanoparticles have an inherent limitation as they are in powder form, which imposes certain unavoidable constraints on their practical applications. Additional challenges include issues related to reuse, preservation, and concerns about potential cytotoxicity to healthy tissues [423]. In a study conducted by Chowdhuri et al., the effectiveness of antibiotic therapy against multidrug-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) was investigated using a zeolite imidazole acid framework (ZIF-8) [434]. This agent, packed with vancomycin and folic acid (targeting ligands), dissolves in an acidic environment, resulting in the rapid release of the encapsulated medicine and causing damage to healthy tissues in the process [435]. To mitigate drug-induced adverse effects, MOF nanoparticles can be placed in a neutral environment, allowing secure encapsulation of the drug within the nanoparticles. Additionally, selecting a drug structure compatible with the MOF nanoparticle composition can further minimize adverse effects [435].

The metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) based on perylenediimide exhibits a remarkable capacity to generate radical anions, rendering extensive chemical modification unnecessary [407]. This inherent property is essential for achieving a high near-infrared (NIR) photothermal conversion efficiency in metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) (η = 52.3%). (Fig. 20
D) Not all MOFs possess photosensitizers or can be utilized as photosensitizers for photodynamic, photothermal, or photocatalytic effects. Some MOFs can undergo modification, such as carbonization or sulfation [436]. The practical and efficient carbonization process enables MOF transformation into photothermal antibacterial materials. For instance, ZnO-doped carbon nanoparticles (ZnO-CNP), known for their excellent photothermal properties, can be synthesized from powdered ZIF-8 nanoparticles by carbonization at 800 °C with argon for two hours, followed by secondary oxidation at 300 °C with oxygen for an additional two hours [429]. Yang et al., enhanced the antibacterial efficacy and reduce toxicity by coating a thermally responsive gel layer (TRGL) onto ZnO-CNP surface [437]. This involved the in situ polymerization of temperature-sensitive poly(n-isopropyl acrylamide) (PNIPAM) layers using cross-linking agents [437]. ZnO-CNP-TRGL was designed with high thermal responsiveness, NIR photo responsiveness, and size-shiftable particle size, specifically targeting localized bacteria through nanocarbon photothermal effects [437]. Yang et al., demonstrated that ZnO-CNP-TRGL with various particle sizes exhibited remarkable photothermal conversion efficiency, controlled release of Zn2+ ions, and acceptable biocompatibility [437].

The material exhibits rapid switching between nanodispersions and micron-sized aggregates upon exposure to near-infrared radiation [437]. This valve-like or gate-like switching capability enables precise and efficient bacterial eradication, making it a smart nanocarbon system. When subjected to near-infrared light, the hybrid nanomaterials generate high temperatures through a photothermal effect [437]. This nanocarbon system prevents nanocarbon entry into healthy cells and circulation, thereby mitigating long-term toxicity to animal organs, damage to healthy cells, and hyperthermia outside the infected site [437]. The temperature-responsive properties of PNIPAM cause photothermal nanocomposites to aggregate, transitioning from hydrophilic to hydrophobic above 40 °C [423]. Subsequent NIR light irradiation further elevates the localized temperature of these aggregates. Moreover, the MOFs release a significant amount of Zn2+ ions, which physically disrupt bacterial membranes and intracellular proteins involved in physiological processes [436,437]. Thus, photothermal therapy is more effective in combating bacteria. The results demonstrate a nearly 100% bactericidal rate at relatively low dosages, comparable to several clinically used antibiotics for multidrug-resistant bacteria [423]. Li et al., observed that the photothermal effect of ZnPB increased the temperature of methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus in localized infection sites at low laser powers, causing disruption to the internal environment and damaging the biofilm [16]. Fig. 20 (A-C).

CuS@HKUST-1 composites were synthesized by Yu et al., through the encapsulation of copper nanoparticles (Cu NPs) within a copper-based metal organic framework (HKUST-1) using the organic ligand 1,3,5-phenylene tricarboxylic acid (H3BTC) [438]. The in situ vulcanization approach facilitated the coordination of HKUST-1 and H3BTC with copper ions, leading to the formation of CuS@HKUST-1 composites with controlled properties. CuS nanoparticles were effectively used in response to near-infrared (NIR) light [439]. CuS NPs exhibited localized surface plasmon resonance, enabling them to absorb NIR light and generate heat through photothermal conversion. This was achieved through the conversion of Cu clusters immobilized in metal organic frameworks (MOFs) to Cu during the in situ sulfidation process, as well as the interaction of CuS with water to produce free radicals via electron exchange [423]. Research demonstrated that exposure to NIR light for 20 min resulted in a bactericidal rate of at least 99.70% and 99.80% against S. aureus and E. coli, respectively [440]. The synergistic actions of Cu ion release, photothermal conversion-induced heat generation, and electron transfer-induced production of free radicals play a vital role in achieving this bactericidal effect. These mechanisms physically disrupt bacteria's active structure, composition, and biofilm, including their cell physiological structure, nucleic acids, and bioactive enzymes [436,441]. Moreover, CuS@HKUST-1 composites exhibited significant biocompatibility with mammals and had manageable negative impacts on the proliferation of healthy tissue cells, even when used over extended periods of time [438]. Their biocompatibility and limited negative effects on healthy tissue cells make them attractive candidates for extended use in biomedical applications. Further research and development are warranted to explore their potential in clinical settings and optimize their performance for targeted antibacterial therapies.

Small-Molecule Based Organic Nanoparticles are simple to manufacture, excellent absorption of near-infrared light, superior in terms of biosafety, Structural controllability, and have a lot of potential for clinical PTT applications [24,442,443]. The most beneficial feature of this nanomaterial for clinical PTT antimicrobial treatment applications is its Structural versatility, optical properties dependent on size, biocompatibility and in vivo degradability, which enables the body to excrete non-toxic decompositions [443–445]. There have been several studies on the usage of Small-Molecule Based Organic Nanoparticle in recent years, including methylene blue (MB), indocyanine green (ICG, a tri-carbocyanine dye), Prussian blue (PB), IR780 and IR825, small molecule dyes(cypate), black phosphorus (BP), and red phosphorus (RB) [1,389,446–452]. Organic small molecule nanoparticles have been extensively researched to develop protective materials in combination with other photothermal nanomaterials. These nanoparticles have been widely reported in biomedical applications, including therapeutic and diagnostic applications for tumors [2,453–456]. The resulting composite nanomaterials have demonstrated good photothermal effects, while also reducing cytotoxicity and tissue damage. However, the photobleaching and thermal degradation of organic small molecule nanoparticles under laser irradiation have limited their clinical applications [457].

ICG (indocyanine green) and PB (prussian blue) are notable examples of small-molecule organic nanoparticles that exhibit strong near-infrared light absorption capabilities [1,452,458,459]. BP (black phosphorus) and RB (rose bengal) have gained prominence in photothermal therapy (PTT) applications due to their favorable photothermal effects and non-toxic breakdown products that facilitate excretion in vivo [446,451,460]. However, organic small-molecule organic nanoparticles have inherent limitations as photothermal agents, including lower photothermal conversion efficiency, hydrophobicity, poor stability, susceptibility to photobleaching, and thermal decomposition upon exposure to laser radiation [461–463]. To address this, Jia et al., constructed the first fine acceptor–donor-acceptor (a-d-a) conjugated framework of organic small molecule photothermal agents, namely CPDT-T, and then encased it in the bi-affinity polymer DSPE-MPEG2000 to create photothermal organic antimicrobial nanoplatforms (CNPs) [424,434]. Following photothermal therapy, the nanoparticles are rapidly cleared from the body, ensuring prompt elimination and avoiding long-term cytotoxicity associated with tissue cell retention. This clearance mechanism enhances the potential to achieve optimal antibacterial effects [463]. Despite their potential, organic small-molecule compounds have not been widely employed in clinical antimicrobial photothermal treatment [464–466]. Most organic small-molecule photothermal agents preferentially utilize the NIR-I (700–900 nm) biological window due to its high photothermal conversion efficiency and NIR laser absorbance for antimicrobial therapy [466]. However, the NIR-II (1000–1350 nm) biological window offers increased tissue penetration depth and greater maximum exposure capacity for antibacterial applications [467]. There is a scarcity of studies on organic photothermal reagents within the NIR-II window, highlighting the need for further investigation into photothermal antibacterial nanoplatforms based on small organic molecules [466]. Carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) exhibit strong light responsiveness and absorbance within the NIR-II biological window, driven by their charge-transfer characteristics, enabling effective bactericidal therapy through local hyperthermia and achieving a high photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE) of approximately 49% with a germicidal rate of nearly 100% against Staphylococcus aureus [444]. Through the membrane physical damage effect brought on by local hyperthermia, CNPs received an extremely successful bactericidal therapy from the NIR-II irradiation for 8 min. CNPs also obtained a photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE) of around 49% and a germicidal rate of almost 100% for Staphylococcus aureus (Gram-positive germs) [444]. Zhu et al., developed lanthanide-based upconversion nanophosphors (Ln-UCNPs) containing organic dyes and polymers for precise photothermal therapy (PTT) in tumor treatment [40].( Fig. 21
A-E) TThe Ln-UCNPs demonstrated excellent biocompatibility, photostability, upconversion luminescence, and photothermal conversion efficiency, enabling effective tumor ablation and inhibition in vivo under near-infrared irradiation [40]. CNPs exhibit superior photothermal conversion capabilities to many other agents, but further research is needed to explore their clinical potential for treating bacterial infections. Miao et al., synthesized a nanocomposite hydrogel (BP/Gel) by doping a hydrogel matrix with black phosphorus (BP) nanosheets [451]. BP/Gel exhibited outstanding NIR photothermal characteristics, stimulated osteogenesis and differentiation of human bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs) in vitro, promoted supporting cell growth for bone regeneration in vivo, and demonstrated excellent antibacterial and tumor cell elimination efficacy, including more than 98% eradication of Staphylococcus aureus.

Nanosystems composed of small-molecule organic nanocomposites, when exposed to near-infrared (NIR) window irradiation, exhibit a remarkable increase in temperature and enhance the synergistic antibacterial effects of photothermal therapy and chemotherapy, particularly against drug-resistant bacteria. In a study by Maaoui et al., poly(vinylpyrrolidone)-encapsulated Prussian blue nanoparticles (PVP-PB NPs) were synthesized as a concentration-dependent bactericidal complex [447]. These PVP-PB NPs demonstrated the ability to effectively eliminate highly pathogenic Escherichia coli and methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus when subjected to NIR light (1 W cm -2). Importantly, PVP-PB NPs select mammalian cells was observed at complex concentrations below 50 μg mL−1 [447]. The findings highlight the potential of small-molecule organic nanocomposites for achieving enhanced antibacterial effects through the combination of photothermal therapy and chemotherapy. The utilization of NIR light allows for targeted and controlled heating of the nanosystems, leading to a substantial temperature rise and effective eradication of drug-resistant bacteria. Moreover, the concentration-dependent nature of the PVP-PBNPs provides an opportunity to selectively target bacterial cells while minimizing adverse effects on mammalian cells. Further exploration of these nanosystems and their optimization for clinical applications could pave the way for advanced antibacterial therapies.

Photothermal agents combined with other antimicrobial agents offer a versatile approach that serves multiple functional purposes. These photothermal agents act as carriers, enabling drug delivery in vivo. They enable targeted binding to focal infection tissues, controlled release of drugs, enhanced antibacterial effects, and improved antibacterial properties. When bacteria are treated with antibiotics alone, they often develop resistance through mechanisms such as blocking drug uptake, accelerating biological metabolism, and increasing drug excretion processes. However, the combination of photothermal therapy (PTT) and antibiotics demonstrates synergistic antibacterial effects that effectively prevent bacterial resistance [468]. In a study by Ma et al., AgNPs and vancomycin were combined on nanoplatforms to create a nanohybrid system, which enhanced antibiotic efficacy against Staphylococcus aureus and E. coli [469]. The physical rupture of bacterial cell walls and the generation of oxygen radicals by AgNPs resulted in a decreased antibiotic requirement. This nanoplatform-based approach effectively killed drug-resistant bacteria, accelerated wound healing, and mitigated the adverse effects associated with high dosages. In vivo trials showed no harm to major organs, highlighting the system's biocompatibility and safety. Another study focused on vancomycin-functionalized magnetic graphene composites (VCM-MCG), where vancomycin was immobilized on the surface of a magnetic chitosan-graphene (MCG) system [66]. This synergistic antimicrobial system combined chemo-antibacterial therapy (CAT) with photothermal antibacterial therapy (PAT) to enhance the overall antimicrobial effect. In both in vitro and in vivo experiments, the MCG system exhibited efficient photothermal conversion upon exposure to near-infrared light, effectively suppressing MRSA and E. coli growth and proliferation. Similarly, the coupling of vancomycin with gold nanoparticles (GNPs) by Huang et al., enhanced the antibacterial activity of vancomycin against vancomycin-resistant enterococci and reduce their resistance to the drug [175]. These studies demonstrate the potential for combining photothermal agents with antimicrobial agents to combat bacterial infections. Synergistic effects achieved through such combinations offer improved therapeutic outcomes, reduced drug resistance, and minimized adverse effects.

The photothermal antimicrobial agent utilizes near-infrared or visible light to generate heat when exposed to light. Photothermal therapy (PTT) is a rapid treatment method that employs physical heat to disrupt bacteria's intracellular protein structure, leading to their eradication [470]. While normal tissue may be affected during therapy, drug-resistant bacteria are prevented [471]. Various types of photothermal agents have been investigated, including precious metal materials, carbon-based nanomaterials, conjugated polymer materials (such as polyaniline (PANI), polydopamine (PDA), poly(3,4-ethylene dioxythiophene) (PEDOT), polypyridine (Ppy)), two-dimensional nanomaterials like black phosphorus (BP) nanosheets and two-dimensional Ti3C2 MXene, as well as emerging single-element two-dimensional materials (e.g., silylene germanyl propylene, stanene), metal complexes, and transition metal sulfides such as copper sulfide (CuS) and molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) [238,396]. These photothermal agents possess desirable characteristics including readily available raw materials, established preparation methods, excellent optical stability, strong light absorption, high photothermal conversion efficiency, and remarkable biocompatibility and histocompatibility [243].

Antimicrobial hydrogels have emerged as a novel form of antibacterial wound dressing due to their water absorption capability, porous structure, bionic properties, and wide-ranging applications [472]. They are being considered as alternatives to antibiotics for bacterial infections and have demonstrated potential for accelerating wound healing [284,473]. Antimicrobial hydrogels can be categorized into five main types: traditional silver-based hydrogels, photoresponsive hydrogels, antibiotic-loaded hydrogels, naturally occurring antibacterial hydrogels, and hydrogels with synergistic effects [473,474]. Li et al., utilized PDA@Cu nanoparticles embedded in a CPAP hydrogel for bactericidal purposes, showing significant in vitro bactericidal activity against Streptococcus aureus (98.2%) and Escherichia coli (100%), as well as high efficiency in vivo bactericidal activity [323]. ( Fig. 22
A-B) In a recent study, Zhao et al., successfully synthesized a biocompatible multifunctional hydrogel dressing (rGB/QCS/PDA-PAM) suitable for dynamic wound studies involving methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) infections [475]. This dressing incorporates modified quaternary carboxymethyl chitosan (QCS) and functionalized graphene (rGB) within a polydopamine-polyacrylamide (PDA-PAM) network. Notably, the incorporation of rGB and QCS in the rGB/QCS/PDA-PAM dressing offers distinct advantages, specifically in terms of enhancing bacterial capture and disrupting bacterial biofilms, thereby significantly improving the photothermal antibacterial efficacy against MRSA [475]. ( Fig. 22 C-D). Furthermore, The presence of covalent and non-covalent crosslinking networks enabled by PDA-PAM plays a crucial role in augmenting the mechanical strength, tissue adhesion, and tissue regeneration-promoting properties of multifunctional hydrogels dressing. ( Fig. 22 C) The findings presented in this study make notable contributions to the advancement of care and treatment strategies for managing multi-resistant infected wounds and offer valuable insights for guiding future research in the field of antimicrobial hydrogels tailored to promoting infected wound healing.

Three-dimensional porous hydrogels have been widely explored as vehicles for hydrophobic medications sustained release. Hydrogels can be physically cross-linked through crosslinkers or chemically cross-linked via covalent bonds. By modifying the shape and molecular mass of the material, distinctive functionalities such as injectability, ductility, biomimetic design, mechanical strength, strong adhesion to solid objects, stimulus-responsive behavior, and tissue regeneration promotion can be achieved. These functionalities can also be introduced by incorporating specific reactive groups or reinforcing agents, including catechol-functionalized groups, electron transfer groups, host–guest supramolecular crosslinkers based on POSS (HGP crosslinker), and functional nanomaterials [476–478]. Due to their resemblance to the extracellular matrix, certain biocompatible hydrogels can accelerate tissue regeneration and possess strong antibacterial properties, making them promising materials for wound dressings. A promising approach involves delivering photothermal antimicrobial agents through hydrogels to the site of local action or targeted eradication of harmful microorganisms [479]. However, when acting at a site of bacterial infection, the photothermal agent may also be taken up by normal cells, circulate in the blood, and accumulate in normal tissues over time [480]. To prevent photothermal agent release, Chen et al., synthesized AuNR-encapsulated graphitic nanocapsules (AuNR@G with improved thermostability) using chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [481]. These nanocapsules were embedded within a poly(vinyl alcohol)/chitosan (CS) hydrogel crosslinked with glutaraldehyde, resulting in the synthesis of AuNR@G-doped poly(vinyl alcohol)/chitosan (CS) (AG-PC) hydrogel. Upon 10 min of laser irradiation at 2 W/cm2, the AG-PC hydrogel demonstrated nearly complete eradication of Gram-negative E and Gram-positive S bacteria due to its porous structure and excellent swelling characteristics.

Composite hydrogel systems have shown significant potential for clinical applications. Liang et al., developed a multifunctional adhesive hydrogel dressing by crosslinking dopamine-transplanted hyaluronic acid (HA-DA) with reduced graphene oxide (rGO) coated with polydopamine (PDA) [482]. This hydrogel dressing exhibits antibacterial properties, promotes complete skin regeneration, provides antioxidant properties, and ensures hemostasis. The hydrogels exhibit superior swelling and degradability, excellent biocompatibility, sustained photothermal activity, NIR-enabled antibacterial activity, and the ability to absorb near-infrared irradiation. In another study, Ko et al., developed a photothermal antimicrobial nanocomposite hydrogel based on poly(3,4-methylenedioxy-thiophene): poly(styrene-sulfonate) (PEDOT: PSS) and agarose [238]. This hydrogel system demonstrates a reversible sol–gel transition in response to temperature changes, superior biocompatibility, thermoplasticity, NIR-induced self-healing properties, and strong antimicrobial activity. Within 100 s of NIR light exposure (808 nm, 2.0 W cm-2), the hydrogel can significantly increase the temperature, and within two minutes, the inactivation rate of Staphylococcus aureus and E. coli reaches approximately 100%. If NIR-responsive or temperature-responsive drug release mechanisms can be achieved, controlled drug release with minimal damage to normal tissues and precise bacterial eradication can be accomplished [40]. The photothermal agents can act precisely at a slight increase in temperature without causing excessive damage to normal tissues [40]. (Fig. 23
A-B) Feng et al., successfully synthesized a photothermal antibacterial hydrogel using agarose, germanene, and chitosan [483]. This germanene hydrogel exhibited remarkable properties, including high-strength drug delivery, pH/NIR-sensitive responsive drug release, and NIR-induced deep penetration, which contributed to the deactivation of residual tumor tissues, bactericidal impact, and wound healing function. In a related study, Cao et al., demonstrated the feasibility of creating various photothermal antimicrobial hydrogels that effectively leveraged the synergistic effects of photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT) [474]. Notably, hydrogels composed of polyacrylamide with Ag@AgCl nanostructures, injectable hydrogels containing dopamine (DA), folic acid (FA), and zinc cross-linked, as well as (CuS/mSiO2) hydrogels formed by cross-linking mesoporous silica (mSiO2) with CuS nanoparticles, exhibited outstanding antibacterial effects against E. coli and S. aureus in antimicrobial testing. These experimental findings signify significant progress in wound healing. Yuan et al., fabricated porous junctional cold gel/silk gel hydrogels (referred to as HNTs@PDA nanohybrid hydrogels) by incorporating dopamine oxidatively polymerized kaolin nanotubes (halloysite@polydopamine) [484]. (Fig. 23 E) These composite hydrogels displayed diminished biological activity against both Gram-negative Escherichia coli and Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus under experimental conditions involving 808 nm near-infrared (NIR) light irradiation. (Fig. 23 D-F) The hydrogels exhibited broad-spectrum photothermal antibacterial capabilities, exceptional biocompatibility, and rapid hemostatic properties. Furthermore, the investigation of angiogenesis-associated (CD31) and inflammation-associated (CD68) cytokines provides valuable information regarding the immunomodulatory and angiogenic effects of these hydrogels [484]. Further research in this field could potentially lead to the development of photothermal antibacterial hydrogel with enhanced antibacterial and tissue-regenerative properties.

Photothermal therapy (PTT) utilizes non-invasive light irradiation to induce a photothermal effect, generating heat that disrupts the structural framework of bacteria, such as biofilms, cell walls, ribosomes, and intracellular active substrates, including enzymes, peptidoglycans, phospholipids, RNA, and proteins [485]. PTT effectively disrupts biofilm integrity and permeability, facilitating the penetration of antimicrobial agents into bacteria protected by biofilms and leading to bacterial cell rupture and death [243,485,486]. Synergistic antimicrobial approaches involve combining multiple antimicrobial methods, which exhibit superior efficacy compared to single treatments.

Due to the high laser intensity and concentration of photothermal agents (PTAs) required for PTT, which may cause irreversible damage to surrounding healthy tissues, synergistic antimicrobial treatments is crucial.
