Numerous studies have proposed multimodal synergistic antibacterial strategies to counteract the negative effects of photothermal therapy and reduce the required power density [468,487]. Multimodal synergistic antimicrobial treatments harness nanocomposite systems, where the controlled generation of heat under infrared light using photothermal effects enables enhanced bacterial eradication [151]. Biofilms' heat-induced permeability facilitates the infiltration of antimicrobials into the bacteria's cells. Additionally, photosensitizers can produce large amounts of reactive oxygen species (ROS), promoting immune cell infiltration and bacterial eradication without inducing bacterial resistance. (Fig. 24
A) Moreover, PTAs can effectively treat bacteria-based infections caused by drug-resistant strains [159]. Yu et al., demonstrated a hydrogen-photothermal synergistic antibacterial approach by incorporating hydrogen into Pd nanocubes, forming Pd-H2 nano-hydrides with active hydrogen [488]. Under near-infrared laser irradiation, the heat generated by Pd nanocubes breaks the bond between Pd and H2, leading to active hydrogen. This on-demand release enhances active hydrogen's antibacterial effect while minimizing damage to healthy tissue. The study showcased the ability to regulate active hydrogen molecules using NIR lasers by varying the laser power density, highlighting the efficacy of hydrogen-photothermal synergy therapy. The Pd-H2 nano-hydrides exhibited high biocompatibility, excellent antimicrobial properties, antibacterial biofilm capability, and improved infected wound healing. The antibacterial mechanism involves the oxidative stress reactive oxygen species (ROS) pathway and the biofilm damage pathway [488]. The oxidative stress ROS pathway upregulates the expression of genes involved in bacterial oxidative metabolism, including narJ, dmpI, and nark, leading to increased ROS production. The photothermal effect also causes thermophysical damage to bacterial biofilms, including organelle membranes, enhancing their permeability, facilitating hydrogen penetration into bacteria, and releasing intracellular bioactive molecules such as RNA and DNA. Nitric oxide (NO) exhibits antibacterial properties and contributes to tissue healing both in vitro and in vivo. NO and photothermal effects combine to achieve synergistic sterilization. Liu et al., conducted a study involving the loading of BNN6@ZIF-8@PDA nanoparticles into a hydrogel matrix (referred to as Gel/BZP hydrogel) for the purpose of near-infrared laser-induced release of NO nanoparticles [489]. In vitro tests demonstrated that exposure to 808 nm NIR laser light resulted in approximately 97% inactivation of S. aureus. Moreover, in an in vivo experiment, full-thickness skin infected with S. aureus was treated with Gel/BZP hydrogel under 808 nm NIR laser radiation for 14 days, leading to complete resolution of the infection. In another study by Zhao et al., a multifunctional platform known as TG-NO-B was developed [28]. This platform exhibited targeted antibacterial activity against wound infections and subcutaneous abscesses through synergistic chemotherapy (NO release) and photothermal therapy (PTT), as illustrated in Fig. 24 B-D. Additionally, the multifunctional platform promoted wound healing, mitigated inflammation in normal tissues, and demonstrated favorable biocompatibility [28]. (Fig. 24 E).

In recent years, there has been a growing interest in exploring the combined therapeutic effects of photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT) by utilizing multimodal nanoplatforms [491]. This development has been facilitated by the discovery of non-overlapping toxicity profiles associated with PDT and PTT. By incorporating both photodynamic and photothermal active agents within a single nanoplatform, researchers aim to harness the synergistic therapeutic effects arising from their combined action. Plasmonic MoO3-x-Ag hybrid nanostructures were synthesized by Yin et al., [492]. These nanostructures exhibit photothermal action, generating heat and thermally charged carriers that result in Ag+ ions release. The sterilization mechanism involves multiple factors, including the release of Ag+ ions, heat production through the photothermal effect (PTT), generation of large amounts of reactive oxygen species (ROS) through photodynamic therapy (PDT), and the formation of thermal holes caused by the separation of thermally charged carriers upon MoO3-x release. The bactericidal activity of the hybrid nanostructures against both gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria was found to be higher compared to using MoO3-x or Ag+ alone. Additionally, the incorporation of MoO3-x in hybrid nanostructures reduces the cost and toxicity associated with Ag nanocubes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reducing the cytotoxicity of photothermal agents
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of nanoparticles and the potential acute/chronic tissue toxicity resulting from the breakdown products of nanocomposite photothermal agents can accumulate in vivo [487]. The investigation of metal nanoparticles for their cytotoxicity and potential side effects has limited their therapeutic applications. Among them, silver nanoparticles (Ag-NPs) and gold nanoparticles (Au-NPs) have received considerable attention in this context. However, by carefully selecting appropriate nanomaterials for nanocomposites development, the cytotoxicity of photothermal agents can be reduced [493]. Over the past few decades, various studies have explored the use of small molecular nanoparticles such as methylene blue (MB), indocyanine green (ICG), and Prussian blue nanoparticles, as well as polymers like polyaniline (PANI), Polydopamine (PDA), polypyrroline (PPy), and poly (3,4-ethylene dioxythiophene) (PEDOT) as organic photothermal agents (PTAs) due to their strong near-infrared light absorption. Among them, Indocyanine green (ICG) is particularly noteworthy [494,495]. These small organic nano-PTAs are readily eliminated from the body shortly after photothermal therapy, minimizing toxicity associated with long-term retention in cellular tissues. Carbon-based PTAs have demonstrated low cytotoxicity while exhibiting antibacterial activity. Another advantage is the tunability of near-infrared light absorption and photothermal conversion by manipulating key parameters such as size, structure, number of layers, and surface chemical groups [48,67]. Carbon-based PTAs' antibacterial mechanisms involve physical damage and chemical stress on bacterial membranes, generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) through oxidative stress, and biochemical electron transfer. Carbon-based PTAs also hold promise as alternatives to antibiotics.

Researchers have employed protective materials to mitigate cytotoxicity in certain studies. Ren et al., synthesized photothermal nanocomposites (GO-HA-AgNPs) by combining hyaluronidase-triggered (HAase-triggered) graphene oxide (GO) with hyaluronic acid (HA) templated AgNPs [496]. The integration of hyaluronic acid (HA) into the nanocomposite allowed for significant antibacterial activity while exhibiting minimal toxicity to mammalian cells. The sterilization activity was achieved through the antimicrobial properties of the AgNPs, the release of Ag+-induced reactive oxygen species (ROS), and the photothermal sterilization effect of GO. In another study, Sun et al., developed the CA@AuMN-HA synergistic system by encapsulating plant essential oils (PEOs) such as cinnamaldehyde (CA) within mesoporous silica-coated gold nanorods (AuMN) [497,498]. This synergistic system demonstrated potent antibacterial efficacy, enabling “on-demand” CA administration and achieving up to 90% suppression of MRSA under 808 nm NIR-laser irradiation. Furthermore, the evaluations revealed minimal impacts on animal cell viability even at high concentrations of 320 μg·mL−1 and no histopathological abnormalities or organ failure. These protective strategies hold promise for reducing cytotoxic effects while enhancing photothermal nanocomposites' antibacterial efficacy.

Fullerenes are a class of carbon-based hollow molecules that can adopt various shapes, including spherical, elliptical, cylindrical, or tubular structures [499,500]. Through various synthetic techniques, such as the evaporation of graphite electrodes, pristine fullerene molecules with highly symmetrical and encapsulating structures can be produced [501]. Moreover, conjugating fullerenes with other molecules can increase bacteria's sensitivity to antimicrobial peptides [502]. Fullerenes find applications in antimicrobial photodynamic inactivation (aPDI) owing to their remarkable ability to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) and singlet oxygen (1O2), high yields of triplet states, excellent photostability, and superior photoactivity [503]. Despite fullerenes' numerous advantages, certain drawbacks limit their broader applications. Firstly, due to their inherent hydrophobicity and limited water solubility, efficient fullerene injection into the targeted area can be challenging. Secondly, fullerene photodynamic processes, which involve ROS and 1O2 generation, can also cause damage to healthy tissues [504]. To address these limitations, surface grafting of different functional groups onto fullerenes, taking advantage of their abundant double bonds, can enhance their water solubility, target specificity, and adsorption capacity. When exposed to 730 nm laser light, fullerene-based nanoparticles were observed to produce stronger thermal signals than antenna-based nanoparticles. Additionally, fullerene-based nanoparticles have been found to be more effective than fullerenes in generating ROS [505]. polyhydroxy fullerenes(PHF) possess desirable properties such as water solubility, high photothermal efficiency, biodegradability, antioxidant properties, and stability, making them excellent candidates for use in photothermal therapy [506]. Previous studies have reported successful application of PHFs in tumor photothermal therapy and imaging [507]. While fullerenes have been studied in photodynamic antibacterial therapy, there is still a lack of experimental evidence to demonstrate their efficacy in photothermal antibacterial therapy [504].

The ideal photothermal nanomaterials should possess certain characteristics, such as high photothermal conversion efficiency and excellent biocompatibility, among others. Bian et al., have developed hierarchical mineralized silver nanocages with hollow silver nanoshells composed of silver nanoparticles modified with octreotide (OCT) template hybridization [508]. The resulting silver nanocages exhibit exceptional surface plasmon resonance (SPR) and near-infrared (NIR) absorption up to 900 nm, allowing for greater photothermal efficacy (PCE up to 46.1%). Moreover, these nanocages offer numerous advantages, such as their structural controllability, excellent cytocompatibility, and superior photothermal stability. The research conducted by Bian et al., has shown that the size and morphology of silver nanocages can be regulated by adjusting the concentration of silver nitrate (AgNO3), which in turn modulates the optical signature local surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) from the visible to the near-infrared (NIR) region [508]. Furthermore, in vivo and in vitro therapeutic dosages using a biological template, without the need for additional toxic surfactants, have demonstrated minimal toxicity. These findings hold significant promise for the development of safe and effective photothermal therapies utilizing silver nanocages. Zhu et al., employed Vapreotide acetate (Vap) as a biotemplate to interact with an AgNO3 solution, which was subsequently reduced with fresh NaBH4 to synthesize biocompatible vaporized peptide-silver nanocages (Vap-AgNCs) [509]. The distinctive nanocage structure of Vap-AgNCs displayed multiple plasmonic bands and superior photothermal conversion efficiency compared to AgNPs when subjected to near-infrared (NIR) irradiation. The silver nanocage described in this study exhibited these characteristics, and its photothermal conversion efficiency is among the top of silver nanobased photothermal agents [508,510]. While previous studies have focused on the use of photothermal therapy for anticancer treatment, we should also explore the potential of this silver nanocage for photothermal antimicrobial applications [160,194,511].

As emerging photothermal antimicrobial nanomaterials, the conjugated oligomeric nanoparticles exhibit efficient near-infrared (NIR) antimicrobial properties, expanding the potential applications of photothermal nanomaterials and providing insights for enhancing photothermal antimicrobial efficiency in other nanomaterials. Zhang et al., synthesized nanoparticles, namely M1-NPs and M2-NPs, using conjugated oligomers, where M2-NPs incorporated an additional acetylene group compared to M1-NPs [512]. Remarkably, the molecular engineering of M2-NPs resulted in several notable advancements. Specifically, M2-NPs exhibited higher mass extinction coefficients, superior light absorption characteristics, enhanced photothermal properties, and improved antimicrobial activity compared to M1-NPs. Furthermore, M2-NPs achieved equivalent sterilization rates to M1-NPs but at lower concentrations. Notably, M2-NPs demonstrated sensitivity towards Gram-positive (G+) bacteria, Gram-negative (G-) bacteria, and fungi. Importantly, M2-NPs exhibited good biocompatibility, causing no harm to normal cells in the absence of light. The broad applicability of M2-NPs, encompassing various domains such as antimicrobial applications and wound treatment, underscores their versatility and potential in diverse fields [512]. Subsequently, the incorporation of polymyxin B (PMB) onto conjugated oligomeric nanoparticles (CON) demonstrated enhanced killing efficacy against drug-resistant Gram-negative bacteria, offering improved strategies for antibiotic applications with reduced PMB dosage [513]. Moreover, the conjugated oligomer nanoparticles (CP-F8P NPs), incorporating strong electron-donating components, exhibited exceptional photothermal conversion efficiency (81.6%) and antimicrobial efficacy (over 99%) even at low concentrations [514]. Specifically, the CP-F8P NPs showcased remarkable antimicrobial activity against Ampr Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus, and Candida albicans [513]. Bismuth-based nanomaterials represent a class of nanostructured materials characterized by their non-toxic nature, favorable X-ray sensitivity, exceptional photothermal conversion efficiency, prolonged half-life, and remarkable near-infrared absorption [515]. These nanomaterials offer the potential for both photothermal and photodynamic therapeutic mechanisms, making them highly promising for advanced multimodal theranostic applications, particularly in the field of cancer research [516]. In a recent study, Li et al., successfully utilized near-infrared-responsive bismuth-based nanomaterials to demonstrate their exceptional antimicrobial capabilities via the combined mechanisms of photothermal and photodynamic therapies [517]. Enhancing the photothermal characteristics of established photothermal materials, exploring the potential of untapped nanomaterials for photothermal experimentation, and advancing the development of novel photothermal materials assume paramount importance in shaping the future progression of the photothermal domain. These endeavors are instrumental in expanding the frontiers of photothermal applications, addressing emerging challenges, and facilitating breakthroughs in various fields reliant on efficient light-to-heat conversion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial mechanism and combined antibacterial strategies
ce:section.ce:para: Photothermal nanomaterials' antibacterial mechanism relies on their ability to absorb near-infrared light and generate a localized hyperthermal environment through the photothermal effect [35,42]. This hyperthermal environment damages bacterial membranes, denaturing proteins, inducing protein leakage, and causing irreversible harm to microorganisms [36,37]. Such mechanisms of bacterial inactivation serve as the foundation for the antimicrobial effect of photothermal nanomaterials as photothermal antimicrobial agents. However, the current development of photothermal nanomaterials, particularly composite materials, has expanded beyond simple localized heating and local action. To enhance the antimicrobial efficacy of photothermal nanomaterials in the photothermal antibacterial process, several strategies have been explored. These include incorporating nanomaterials targeting [53], carrier transport capacity [342], tissue regeneration promotion [475], controlled release systems [212], physical contact/capture/kill mechanisms [63,117], activation of ROS-mediated oxidative damage(PDA) [279], Enhancement of antibiotics and antibacterial peptide efficacy [66,136], and stimulation of cytokine and vascular factor release [279,484] ( Table 5 ). Experimental studies investigating these strategies have been extensively conducted, yielding satisfactory results in terms of enhanced sterilization rates.

This extension of the photothermal antimicrobial mechanism represents the exploration of additional functions that can augment the antibacterial efficacy of the photothermal effect. It can also harness other antimicrobial agents' sterilization capabilities. Crucially, the inactivation of multi-drug resistant strains is of the utmost importance as we prepare for the “post-antibiotic era.” Embracing this era necessitates reducing the risk of microbial harm to human health and fostering advancements in medical progress. These studies exemplify the effectiveness of photothermal nanomaterials in combating antibiotic-resistant strains and driving forward antimicrobial strategies. By expanding the scope of their antimicrobial mechanisms and optimizing their multifunctional properties, photothermal nanomaterials contribute to the mitigation of microbial threats, thereby promoting overall medical advancements and safeguarding human health.

Photothermal therapy (PTT) has emerged as a highly promising modality in antimicrobial therapy, particularly for tackling antibiotic-resistant bacteria. This promising approach owes its success to rapid advancements in the development of various photothermal nanoparticles. This article discusses the PTT antibacterial uses and antimicrobial properties of carbon-based nanoparticles, noble metal nanomaterials, metal sulfide/oxide nanomaterials, organic small molecule nanomaterials, organometallic frameworks, polymeric nanomaterials, and MXenes-based materials. Notably, numerous nanomaterials have exhibited remarkable photothermal antibacterial properties during the laboratory research phase. As these nanomaterials continue to find extensive applications in diverse areas, there is a pressing need to further enhance the production and purification procedures associated with these materials. Advancement of these procedures will ensure efficient and safe nanomaterial utilization in various applications. Continued research and development efforts in this domain are crucial for the realization of the full breakthroughs of photothermal therapy in combating antibiotic-resistant bacteria and addressing the challenges posed by microbial infections.

CBNs possess a range of advantageous properties, including a wide source range, low cost, environmental friendliness, high temperature stability, photothermal conversion capability, modifiable photothermal properties, and high thermal conductivity. However, in their original form, CBNs exhibit relatively low photothermal effects in terms of antimicrobial activity. Therefore, it is necessary to either compound them with other materials or modify and tune their properties to fully exploit their potential. To enhance the photothermal effect and antibacterial impact, noble metal nanoparticles can be modified by altering their structural form, size, and nanomorphology, capitalizing on localized surface plasmon resonance. By combining precious metal nanoparticles with other agents such as graphene nanosheets and polymer hydrogels, flexible photothermal antibacterial composites can be synthesized. In the realm of photothermal nanomaterials, metal complexes like copper sulfide nanoparticles and molybdenum sulfide nanosheets are preferred due to their abundant raw material sources, cost-effective preparation, excellent product stability, and notable biocompatibility. Conjugated polymeric photothermal nanomaterials, such as polyaniline and polydopamine, offer flexibility and can be functionalized or complexed with other antimicrobial agents, further expanding their application potential. MXenes-based materials have shown remarkable potential as photothermal agents (PTAs) or photosensitizers (PSs) for rapid sterilization, disinfection, and other related fields. This is attributed to their photothermal or photodynamic characteristics and the abundance of active sites involved in chemical reactions. MOFs (Metal-Organic Frameworks) and MOFs-based nanocomposites, known for their non-invasive, tunable, and porous structure, exhibit strong antibacterial properties. They can generate reactive oxygen radicals upon light and/or high-temperature exposure, leading to bacterial eradication. Additionally, they employ various antimicrobial mechanisms, as discussed earlier. While MOFs demonstrate excellent antibacterial capabilities, challenges remain. These include the stability and repeatability of composite materials, which can increase their cost. They also include the economical production of MOFs, the in vivo degradation and toxicity of MOFs themselves, as well as the toxicity and metabolism of their derivatives. Organic small-molecule nanomaterials, on the other hand, offer advantages in terms of easy integration into other nanomaterials and the ability to undergo chemical modifications with functional groups. These properties enhance their versatility and potential applications in antimicrobial research. The field of photothermal therapy (PTT) for antimicrobial applications has witnessed the emergence of organic small-molecule nanomaterials, which exhibit minimal or even non-toxic cytotoxicity. These materials can be effectively eliminated from the body through catabolism, reducing the potential cumulative harmful effects associated with long-term use. Organic small-molecule photothermal agents (PTAs) hold a strong promise for clinical applications in photothermal antimicrobials. However, comparing the sterilization effectiveness or photothermal conversion efficiency of different photothermal nanomaterials necessitates intricate research investigations. This complexity arises from various factors, including diverse experimental data and study conditions. Parameters such as nanoparticle dispersing concentration, nanocomposite component design, selection of visible light, power density of near-infrared (NIR) window illumination, and radiation duration can significantly vary among studies. Obtaining comprehensive and accurate experimental data for future comparisons is challenging. To facilitate fair and meaningful comparisons of sterilization or photothermal conversion performance among different nanomaterials, it may be necessary to establish a standardized set of trials in the future. Such standardization efforts would contribute to the advancement of the field. They would also enable more reliable assessments of the efficacy of various photothermal nanomaterials for antimicrobial applications.

Further research is essential to assess the long-term biosafety of photothermal nanomaterials. Recent studies have made progress in minimizing nanomaterial detrimental effects on adjacent normal tissue, which is a positive development. Accurate quantification of photothermal therapy heat is crucial to effectively destroy focal tissue and pathogens while minimizing harm to surrounding normal cells. Precise measurement and control of the thermal effects are essential for optimizing the therapeutic outcome. It is crucial to consider the potential impact of the breakdown products of these photothermal nanomaterials, as they may accumulate in the body and cause acute or chronic tissue toxicity. Therefore, evaluating the material's degradation effects on immune function and cytotoxicity to normal tissue is of the utmost importance. Long-term experimental research is still necessary for the successful development of photothermal nanomaterials. In addition to their applications in antibacterial research, photothermal nanomaterials hold significant promise for a wide range of clinical therapies in medicine.
